proposals.title: Evaluating the Feasibility of Siderophore-Antibiotic Conjugates for Overcoming Resistance in Gram-positive Pathogens: An In Silico and In Vitro Proof-of-Concept Study
proposals.motivation: The rise of multidrug-resistant (MDR) Gram-positive pathogens, particularly methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and vancomycin-resistant Enterococci (VRE), represents a critical public health threat. Current treatment options are increasingly limited, and novel mechanisms to deliver antibiotics into resistant bacteria are urgently needed. Siderophore-antibiotic conjugates (SACs) have shown promise for Gram-negative infections by exploiting iron-uptake pathways, but their potential against Gram-positive organisms remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the siderophore-mediated 'Trojan horse' drug delivery mechanism can be adapted to target resistant Gram-positive pathogens by designing conjugates that utilize their distinct iron acquisition systems, thereby enhancing the intracellular accumulation and efficacy of existing antibiotics.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a computational analysis of known Gram-positive siderophore receptors (e.g., from S. aureus and E. faecium) to identify potential ligand scaffolds. Using this data, we will design and synthesize novel conjugates linking these scaffolds to antibiotics with known Gram-positive activity but compromised by resistance (e.g., a beta-lactam or oxazolidinone). In vitro efficacy will be tested against a panel of MDR Gram-positive clinical isolates (including MRSA and VRE) using standard broth microdilution MIC assays, with comparisons to the parent antibiotic alone. Mechanistic studies will include iron-competition assays and intracellular antibiotic concentration measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic assessment of the siderophore conjugate strategy for Gram-positive bacteria, establishing a foundational proof-of-concept. It will identify promising conjugate scaffolds and generate critical preliminary data on their spectrum and potential to overcome specific resistance mechanisms, guiding future drug development efforts.
proposals.novelty: While siderophore conjugates are an established concept for Gram-negatives, their application to Gram-positive pathogens is novel and non-obvious due to fundamental differences in cell wall structure and iron acquisition biology. This proposal directly challenges the assumption that the mechanism is limited to Gram-negative spectra.

proposals.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis to Define the Role of Cefiderocol in the Treatment of Carbapenem-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Framework for Clinical Guideline Development
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a novel siderophore cephalosporin with potent activity against carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative pathogens, including Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Enterobacterales. However, its optimal place in therapy relative to newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam) and other novel agents (e.g., sulbactam-durlobactam) remains undefined, leading to variable and potentially suboptimal prescribing. Clear, evidence-based guidance is urgently needed to preserve its efficacy and ensure appropriate use in the face of rising antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic synthesis of available clinical trial and real-world evidence will identify specific infection types, pathogen profiles, and patient subgroups (e.g., renal impairment, critically ill) for which cefiderocol demonstrates superior or non-inferior efficacy and safety compared to other last-line agents, enabling the creation of a structured treatment algorithm.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines. A comprehensive search of PubMed, EMBASE, and clinical trial registries (pre-2026) will identify all clinical studies on cefiderocol. Data on clinical/microbiological outcomes, mortality, adverse events, and patient/pathogen characteristics will be extracted. Comparative efficacy against other agents will be analyzed using network meta-analysis where possible. Subgroup analyses will be performed based on infection source, pathogen (including specific resistance mechanisms), and patient comorbidities. The synthesized evidence will be formatted into a proposed treatment decision framework using GRADE methodology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative synthesis of cefiderocol's clinical performance across key resistant pathogens and clinical scenarios. It will directly inform the development of institutional treatment guidelines and antimicrobial stewardship programs by offering an evidence-based hierarchy for last-line antibiotic selection. The proposed framework will help clinicians optimize therapy for the most challenging infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior reviews describe cefiderocol's microbiology, this study uniquely focuses on synthesizing comparative clinical effectiveness data to create a practical treatment algorithm, integrating patient subgroup analysis—a method highlighted in Dr. Pogue's recent work on ceftazidime-avibactam vs. ceftolozane-tazobactam—to personalize placement in therapy.

proposals.title: Design, Synthesis, and In Vitro Evaluation of Catechol-Conjugated Fluoroquinolones and Aminoglycosides for Overcoming Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections
proposals.motivation: The rise of multidrug-resistant (MDR) Gram-negative pathogens, particularly those with reduced outer membrane permeability and upregulated efflux pumps, has rendered many non-β-lactam antibiotics ineffective. The siderophore-antibiotic conjugate strategy, exemplified by cefiderocol, exploits bacterial iron-uptake pathways to bypass these resistance mechanisms, but its application has been largely confined to β-lactam cores. Extending this Trojan horse approach to other antibiotic classes is a critical, unmet need to expand the therapeutic arsenal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that covalently fusing a catechol siderophore moiety to fluoroquinolone (e.g., ciprofloxacin) and aminoglycoside (e.g., tobramycin) core structures will facilitate active uptake via bacterial iron transport systems, thereby enhancing their potency against MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii clinical isolates where conventional agents fail.
proposals.method: We will employ rational medicinal chemistry to synthesize novel conjugates, linking a catechol-chelator via a stable, enzymatically cleavable linker to the primary amine of ciprofloxacin and a hydroxyl group of tobramycin. Conjugates will be screened for in vitro antibacterial activity against a panel of well-characterized, pre-2026 MDR clinical isolates (including carbapenem-resistant strains) using standardized broth microdilution methods under iron-depleted conditions. Uptake mechanism will be probed using competition assays with excess free siderophore and in transporter-deficient mutant strains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic proof-of-concept that the siderophore-mediated delivery strategy can be successfully applied to revive older, off-patent antibiotic classes. It will yield novel chemical entities with defined structure-activity relationships and generate critical preclinical data on spectrum and potential resistance liabilities, informing future lead optimization and combination therapy studies.
proposals.novelty: While siderophore conjugation is established for β-lactams, its application to fluoroquinolones and aminoglycosides is underexplored. This proposal uniquely focuses on designing cleavable linkers tailored to the distinct pharmacophores and intracellular targets of these non-β-lactam classes, moving beyond simple appendage to rationally engineer pro-drug activation.

proposals.title: Evaluating the In Vitro Activity and Synergy of Meropenem in Combination Regimens Against Extensively Drug-Resistant Gram-Negative Pathogens: A Pharmacodynamic Analysis
proposals.motivation: The rise of extensively drug-resistant (XDR) Gram-negative pathogens, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii, represents a critical threat to global health. Meropenem is frequently retained in combination regimens for such infections based on historical susceptibility and theoretical synergy, but its true contribution in the context of high-level, defined resistance mechanisms is unclear. Determining if meropenem provides any additive benefit is essential to optimize therapy, reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure, and guide antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: For XDR Gram-negative pathogens with confirmed meropenem resistance (MIC ≥16 µg/mL), meropenem provides no intrinsic bactericidal activity and demonstrates no synergistic or additive effect when combined with other active agents, even under optimized, high-dose pharmacodynamic exposure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic in vitro study using a curated collection of clinically derived XDR P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii isolates with characterized resistance mechanisms (e.g., metallo-beta-lactamases, serine carbapenemases). Primary methods include: 1) Standard broth microdilution to confirm baseline MICs to meropenem and comparator agents (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, sulbactam-durlobactam). 2) Time-kill synergy assays evaluating meropenem alone and in combination at clinically achievable, high-dose exposures (simulating prolonged infusions). 3) Pharmacodynamic modeling using the hollow-fiber infection model (HFIM) for select strains to assess bacterial kill and resistance suppression over 7 days under dynamic, human-simulated meropenem concentrations in combination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, mechanism-based evidence on the utility of meropenem in XDR infections, directly informing clinical practice and stewardship guidelines. It will generate a framework for de-escalation decisions in complex resistance scenarios and contribute high-quality pharmacodynamic data to the understanding of combination therapy for XDR pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While meropenem resistance is well-documented, its persistent use in combination for XDR infections is common. This study directly challenges this practice by rigorously testing for synergy under optimized dosing conditions, moving beyond observational clinical data to definitive mechanistic pharmacodynamic evidence.

proposals.title: Clinical Failure as a Superior Primary Endpoint for Antibiotic Trials in Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Retrospective Analysis of CZA vs. C/T Studies
proposals.motivation: Mortality is a common but insensitive primary endpoint in antibiotic trials for MDR infections, as death is often influenced by comorbidities and occurs infrequently in non-bacteremic cases. This can obscure true differences in antibiotic efficacy, complicating clinical decision-making and regulatory evaluation. A patient-centered endpoint like clinical failure, encompassing persistent symptoms, microbiological failure, or need for new antibiotics, may better reflect real-world treatment success.
proposals.hypothesis: In comparative effectiveness studies of antibiotics for MDR Gram-negative infections, clinical failure will demonstrate greater statistical power and clinical relevance than all-cause mortality as a primary endpoint, revealing significant treatment differences that mortality endpoints miss.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, patient-level data analysis leveraging existing datasets from studies like CACTUS and other trials of ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA) and ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T). A composite clinical failure endpoint will be defined as a composite of 30-day all-cause mortality, microbiological failure, recurrence, or escalation/changes in antibiotic therapy. Using statistical power simulations, we will compare the sample sizes required to detect a clinically meaningful difference between treatment arms using the composite failure endpoint versus 30-day mortality alone.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for endpoint selection in future antibiotic trials, potentially increasing trial efficiency and clinical relevance. It will offer specific, evidence-based guidance to clinicians, researchers, and regulatory bodies on interpreting comparative effectiveness for MDR infections.
proposals.novelty: While composite endpoints are discussed, this proposal directly tests and quantifies the superiority of a clinically-defined failure endpoint over mortality in real-world comparative effectiveness data for leading anti-pseudomonal agents, moving beyond theoretical discussion to actionable evidence.

proposals.title: Impact of Rapid Molecular Diagnostics on Antibiotic Selection and Clinical Outcomes in Patients with Extensively Drug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center Stepped-Wedge Trial
proposals.motivation: Empiric broad-spectrum combination therapy for suspected extensively drug-resistant (XDR) Gram-negative infections drives antimicrobial overuse, toxicity, and resistance. Pre-2026, novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations like ceftazidime-avibactam and sulbactam-durlobactam are critical yet expensive tools, but their optimal use requires precise pathogen identification and resistance profiling. Rapid diagnostic platforms (e.g., multiplex PCR, genotypic resistance detection) are commercially available, but their real-world impact on de-escalation and patient outcomes in XDR infections remains inadequately quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: The implementation of a stewardship protocol mandating review of rapid molecular diagnostic results within 12 hours of blood culture positivity will lead to faster, more targeted antibiotic therapy (reduced use of empiric combination regimens) without compromising clinical efficacy for patients with XDR Gram-negative infections.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across 10-15 US hospitals with high rates of MDR/XDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii. The intervention is a stewardship bundle: BioFire® Blood Culture Identification 2 panel or similar rapid diagnostic, coupled with a pharmacist (PharmD)-led alert system and a treatment algorithm. Control periods use conventional culture and susceptibility methods. Primary outcome is time to optimal targeted therapy. Secondary outcomes include days of combination therapy, clinical cure at day 14, mortality, and cost. Analysis will use generalized linear mixed models, adjusting for clustering and patient-level confounders (e.g., source of infection, severity).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence on whether rapid diagnostics can safely reduce broad-spectrum antibiotic use in the highest-risk infections, directly informing stewardship guidelines. It will generate actionable data on the real-world clinical and economic value of these technologies for health systems and payers, moving beyond observational studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., CACTUS) compares antibiotic efficacy in subgroups, this trial prospectively tests a diagnostic-driven stewardship *strategy* for XDR pathogens, focusing on de-implementation of unnecessary combination therapy rather than comparing individual drug regimens.

proposals.title: Clinical Outcomes in Patients with Extensively Drug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections Managed with Supportive and Immunomodulatory Care in the Absence of In Vitro Active Antimicrobials: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The rise of pan-resistant bacterial infections, particularly carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, presents a dire clinical scenario where no antimicrobials demonstrate reliable in vitro activity. Current guidelines and clinical trials, such as those for sulbactam-durlobactam or ceftazidime-avibactam, focus on developing new active agents, but a critical gap exists in understanding outcomes when such agents are unavailable. This study addresses the urgent, real-world question of whether aggressive supportive care and adjunctive immunomodulation can lead to survival in these otherwise untreatable infections.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with extensively drug-resistant (XDR) Gram-negative bacterial infections where no antimicrobials show in vitro activity, a protocol of optimized supportive care (including source control, organ support, and nutritional optimization) combined with adjunctive immunomodulatory strategies (e.g., granulocyte colony-stimulating factor [G-CSF], intravenous immunoglobulin [IVIG]) will result in a non-inferior rate of clinical cure at 30 days compared to a historical control cohort of patients with infections susceptible to last-resort agents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study using data from US hospitals (including Michigan-based networks from prior work) from 2020-2025. The intervention cohort will be adults with culture-confirmed XDR Gram-negative infections (MICs indicating resistance to all standard tested agents) managed without in vitro active antibiotics. Using propensity score matching (PSM) with a greedy nearest-neighbor algorithm, we will match them 1:2 to a control cohort with infections susceptible to last-line agents (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam). The primary outcome is clinical cure (composite of survival, resolution of signs/symptoms, and no escalation of care) at 30 days. Secondary analyses will use multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of success within the intervention group, focusing on specific supportive and immunomodulatory interventions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robust, comparative evidence on clinical outcomes for a protocolized non-antibiotic management strategy in the era of pan-resistance. It will inform the development of clinical guidelines for these desperate cases and shift the paradigm from a sole focus on novel antimicrobial development to a more holistic, host-directed therapeutic approach. The findings will directly support antimicrobial stewardship and consultant recommendations in complex cases.
proposals.novelty: While existing research, including Dr. Pogue's work on subgroup efficacy of novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors, focuses on comparing active antimicrobials, this proposal directly challenges the necessity of any in vitro active agent. It systematically tests whether host-directed therapy can be a viable alternative when microbiology reports indicate no options, a question largely unaddressed in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Pharmacometric and Clinical Outcomes Analysis to Optimize Dosing and Duration of Ceftazidime-Avibactam and Aztreonam Combination Therapy for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections, particularly those producing metallo-beta-lactamases (MBLs), represent a critical threat with limited, effective treatment options. The combination of ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA) and aztreonam (ATM) is a promising salvage therapy, but its clinical application is hindered by a lack of evidence-based, optimized dosing regimens and treatment durations, leading to potential underdosing, toxicity, and suboptimal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacometric model-informed dosing regimen of CZA+ATM, tailored to specific patient subgroups (e.g., renal impairment, obesity) and infection sites, will achieve superior pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) target attainment and be associated with improved clinical cure rates and reduced 30-day mortality compared to standard, non-optimized dosing.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients treated with CZA+ATM for CRE infections. Using electronic health record data, we will perform population pharmacokinetic (PopPK) modeling (using NONMEM) to characterize drug exposure. We will then use Monte Carlo simulations to assess the probability of target attainment (PTA) for various dosing regimens against CRE MIC distributions. Clinical outcomes (cure, mortality, recurrence) will be analyzed using multivariable regression, and an exposure-response analysis will link PK/PD targets to efficacy. The final output will be a patient-stratified dosing nomogram.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, patient-subgroup-specific dosing recommendations for CZA+ATM combination therapy against CRE, directly addressing a critical gap in antimicrobial stewardship. It will offer clinicians a practical tool to maximize efficacy while minimizing toxicity and resistance emergence, moving beyond the current 'one-size-fits-all' approach.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies like CACTUS have compared antibiotic efficacy in subgroups, this proposal uniquely integrates high-fidelity PopPK modeling, extensive simulation, and clinical outcomes data specifically for the complex CZA+ATM combination to generate actionable, optimized dosing guidance, a step beyond observational comparative effectiveness.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center Trial Comparing Ceftazidime-Avibactam Monotherapy versus Combination Regimens for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections: The COMBAT-CRE Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical public health threat with high mortality rates. While novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor agents like ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA) are first-line monotherapies, emerging resistance and the theoretical benefit of synergistic combinations necessitate a definitive clinical comparison. Current guidelines, based largely on retrospective data and expert opinion, lack high-level evidence to recommend for or against combination therapy, leading to variable and potentially suboptimal prescribing.
proposals.hypothesis: For the treatment of serious CRE infections, combination therapy with CZA plus a second active agent (e.g., aztreonam, fosfomycin, or a polymyxin) will result in superior clinical cure and lower 30-day all-cause mortality compared to CZA monotherapy.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, randomized, open-label, multi-center clinical trial. Adult hospitalized patients with confirmed CRE infections (bacteremia, pneumonia, complicated UTI) will be randomized 1:1 to receive CZA monotherapy or CZA plus a second agent selected based on pre-specified susceptibility criteria and a standardized institutional protocol. The primary endpoint will be a composite of clinical cure at test-of-cure and 30-day all-cause mortality. Secondary endpoints include microbiological eradication, emergence of resistance, and safety. A pre-planned subgroup analysis will assess outcomes in key populations, such as those with bacteremia or high-risk resistance mechanisms (e.g., metallo-beta-lactamases), building on Dr. Pogue's prior subgroup methodology from the CACTUS study.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-quality, prospective evidence to guide therapeutic decisions for CRE infections, directly informing clinical guidelines and stewardship programs. It will define the patient populations and infection types that benefit most from combination therapy, potentially improving survival and curbing resistance. The findings will also establish a framework for evaluating future antimicrobial combinations in a rigorous, patient-centered trial design.
proposals.novelty: While retrospective studies and meta-analyses have explored this question, this proposal represents a novel, prospective, randomized trial specifically designed to compare modern, guideline-recommended monotherapy (CZA) against rationally selected combination regimens in a pragmatic clinical setting, with embedded subgroup analyses to personalize future recommendations.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Phenotypic Profiling of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) to Decipher Heteroresistance and Tolerance Driving Treatment Failure
proposals.motivation: The clinical failure of novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations like ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA) against CRE is often attributed to heteroresistance, where a resistant subpopulation survives treatment, and antibiotic tolerance, a non-genetic survival mechanism. Standard population-level MIC testing fails to detect these minority subpopulations, leading to suboptimal antibiotic selection and poor patient outcomes, as seen in studies of CZA vs. ceftolozane-tazobactam for MDR Pseudomonas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within clonal CRE populations classified as susceptible by clinical breakpoints, distinct single-cell phenotypic subpopulations exhibiting heteroresistance or tolerance to last-resort agents (e.g., CZA, meropenem-vaborbactam) are the primary drivers of regrowth and treatment failure in vitro.
proposals.method: We will employ microfluidics-based mother machine platforms or droplet microfluidics to track the growth and lysis of individual CRE cells (clinical isolates from the CACTUS study cohort) under prolonged, sub-MIC antibiotic exposure. Time-lapse microscopy will be combined with fluorescent viability stains (e.g., SYTOX Green) and reporter constructs for stress responses. Single-cell growth rates, division times, and survival durations will be quantified using custom image analysis pipelines in Python (using libraries like scikit-image and TrackMate) to classify phenotypes as susceptible, tolerant, or heteroresistant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-resolution, mechanistic map of single-cell survival strategies in CRE, directly linking specific phenotypic subpopulations to the failure of contemporary antibiotics. It will generate a framework for 'phenotypic MIC' profiling that could inform more predictive diagnostics and rational combination therapy to suppress resistant subpopulations.
proposals.novelty: While bulk studies of heteroresistance exist, applying continuous single-cell tracking specifically to CRE exposed to the latest beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors (like sulbactam-durlobactam) to dissect the dynamics between tolerance and heteroresistance is novel and addresses a critical gap identified in recent clinical efficacy studies.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic Optimization of Beta-Lactam/Beta-Lactamase Inhibitor Therapy: A Simulation Study of Separate-Infusion vs. Fixed-Dose Combination Dosing in Critically Ill Patients
proposals.motivation: Fixed-dose combinations (FDCs) of beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors (BL/BLIs) simplify administration but lock the pharmacokinetic (PK) ratio of the components. In critically ill patients with altered PK (e.g., augmented renal clearance, renal impairment), this fixed ratio may lead to suboptimal drug exposure, potentially driving treatment failure or resistance. Pre-2026, therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) for beta-lactams is gaining traction, yet FDCs inherently limit its utility for optimizing the inhibitor component independently.
proposals.hypothesis: Separate, PK-guided administration of the beta-lactam and beta-lactamase inhibitor components will achieve target pharmacodynamic (PD) exposures for both agents in a greater proportion of simulated patient phenotypes compared to standard fixed-dose combination regimens.
proposals.method: We will develop a population pharmacokinetic (popPK) model using published pre-2026 data for ceftazidime and avibactam. Using Monte Carlo simulation (e.g., with R/`mrgsolve`), we will simulate 10,000 virtual patients across diverse renal function subgroups. We will compare standard FDC dosing against a strategy where avibactam is dosed separately based on estimated renal function to maintain a free drug concentration above a predefined threshold (e.g., fT>MIC for the beta-lactam, fT>threshold for the inhibitor). The primary outcome is the probability of target attainment (PTA) for both components simultaneously.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, model-based framework to challenge the dogma that FDCs are always preferable. It will offer specific, data-driven dosing recommendations for separate BL/BLI administration tailored to patient renal function, directly supporting the development of more flexible TDM protocols. The findings will inform clinical trial design and stewardship guidelines for multidrug-resistant Gram-negative infections.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD optimization of beta-lactams is established, this proposal specifically targets the inflexibility of the BL/BLI *ratio* in FDCs, a recognized but under-modeled problem. It moves beyond descriptive prescribing pattern analysis (as in Pogue's sinusitis work) to a predictive, simulation-based evaluation of a novel dosing paradigm.

proposals.title: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis of Microbiological Eradication as a Predictor of Clinical Cure in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections Treated with Novel β-Lactam/β-Lactamase Inhibitor Combinations
proposals.motivation: Clinical cure remains the standard primary endpoint for antimicrobial trials, but for multidrug-resistant infections like CRE, clinical outcomes can be confounded by comorbidities and source control. There is a critical pre-2026 debate on whether microbiological endpoints, such as eradication or suppression of resistance, are more sensitive and specific measures of antibiotic efficacy for these pathogens. This study directly addresses this gap by analyzing real-world data to inform future trial design.
proposals.hypothesis: For CRE infections treated with novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam), microbiological eradication by end of therapy is a stronger and more reliable predictor of long-term clinical success (e.g., 30-day survival, absence of relapse) than composite clinical cure assessments at test-of-cure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record and microbiological data from U.S. hospitals (2018-2024). Adult patients with documented CRE infections treated with a novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor will be included. The primary analysis will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for key confounders (e.g., APACHE-II, source of infection, renal function), to model the association between microbiological eradication (primary exposure) and a composite clinical failure endpoint (primary outcome). Secondary analyses will employ Cox proportional hazards models to assess time-to-event outcomes and evaluate the emergence of resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, real-world evidence on the relationship between microbiological and clinical outcomes in CRE infections, directly informing regulatory and clinical discussions on optimal trial endpoints. It will offer a framework for assessing novel antibiotics where traditional clinical endpoints may be insufficient. The findings will guide more precise antibiotic development and stewardship for multidrug-resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies like CACTUS have compared clinical efficacy between antibiotics, this proposal uniquely focuses on validating a microbiological endpoint as a superior surrogate in the specific, high-mortality context of CRE, leveraging recent real-world data on the latest approved agents.

proposals.title: Real-World Effectiveness, Safety, and Resistance Dynamics of Meropenem-Vaborbactam for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Infections: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a critical new agent for carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections, approved based on the TANGO II trial. However, real-world data on its long-term clinical outcomes, safety in diverse, complex patients (e.g., renal impairment, immunocompromised), and the potential for on-treatment resistance emergence remain limited. Post-marketing surveillance is essential to validate trial efficacy and inform optimal use in the face of this urgent antimicrobial resistance threat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-world clinical success rates for MEV will be comparable to those in the TANGO II trial but will vary significantly across patient subgroups defined by infection source, comorbidities, and prior antibiotic exposure. We further hypothesize that treatment-emergent resistance to MEV, while rare, will be associated with specific bacterial mechanisms and patient risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients treated with MEV for CRE infections from 2018-2025. Data on demographics, microbiology, treatment details, and outcomes will be collected via a standardized electronic case report form. The primary outcome is a composite of clinical success (survival, resolution of signs/symptoms, no MEV re-initiation). We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of success and failure. All available post-therapy isolates will undergo broth microdilution testing for MEV MICs and whole-genome sequencing to identify resistance mechanisms (e.g., KPC variant evolution, porin mutations, efflux pump upregulation).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the largest and most comprehensive real-world evidence on MEV's effectiveness and safety profile outside of clinical trials. It will generate actionable data on which patient populations derive the greatest benefit and quantify the risk of resistance emergence, directly informing treatment guidelines and antimicrobial stewardship programs.
proposals.novelty: While other studies have reported initial real-world experiences, this proposal systematically investigates long-term outcomes and couples detailed clinical data with genomic analysis of serial isolates to directly link patient-level factors with the molecular evolution of resistance during MEV therapy.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Clinical Trial of Nanopore Sequencing for Real-Time Genotypic Resistance Prediction in Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: The rise of multidrug-resistant (MDR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii necessitates rapid, precise antibiotic selection to improve outcomes and combat resistance. Current phenotypic susceptibility testing requires 48-72 hours, forcing clinicians to rely on broad-spectrum, often suboptimal empiric therapy. Technologies developed for rapid viral genomic identification, like portable nanopore sequencing, offer a potential paradigm shift for bacterial infections but lack robust clinical validation for antibiotic decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a point-of-care nanopore sequencing workflow directly from positive blood culture bottles will accurately predict key resistance genotypes (e.g., blaKPC, blaNDM, blaOXA-48-like, ESBLs) and enable antibiotic regimen adjustments within 8 hours, leading to more frequent optimal definitive therapy compared to standard of care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, single-center trial enrolling patients with monomicrobial bacteremia due to MDR Gram-negative rods. Positive blood cultures will be processed using a rapid extraction kit and sequenced on a MinION Mk1C device. We will employ the EPI2ME platform with a customized bioinformatics workflow (including ARMA for resistance gene detection and a local BLAST database for variant calling) to identify resistance determinants. A clinical algorithm, developed with antimicrobial stewardship input, will translate genotypes to predicted phenotypes (e.g., 'ceftazidime-avibactam susceptible' for blaKPC). The primary outcome is the proportion of patients receiving optimal definitive therapy within 24 hours of culture positivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical real-world evidence on the feasibility, accuracy, and clinical impact of rapid genomic diagnostics for antibiotic stewardship in high-risk infections. It will generate a validated, implementable protocol for integrating sequencing into stewardship workflows and offer novel data on genotype-phenotype correlations for newer agents like sulbactam-durlobactam and ceftolozane-tazobactam.
proposals.novelty: While nanopore sequencing has been used in research, this proposal applies it pragmatically at the point-of-care for real-time therapeutic guidance, specifically bridging virology-derived rapid diagnostics to bacterial resistance, an area with limited pre-2026 clinical trial data. The focus on creating an actionable clinical algorithm, rather than just reporting genomic data, is a key innovation.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Synergy Screening to Identify Novel Antibiotic Combinations Against Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE)
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical public health threat with limited, often toxic, treatment options. The success of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV demonstrates the power of systematic synergy screening to overcome resistance, but this approach has not been fully translated to CRE. A systematic, high-throughput method to identify novel, potent antibiotic combinations is urgently needed to expand the therapeutic arsenal.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying high-throughput, matrix-based synergy screening platforms, analogous to those used in HIV drug development, will identify novel, clinically actionable antibiotic combinations with synergistic activity against a diverse panel of CRE clinical isolates.
proposals.method: We will employ a checkerboard broth microdilution assay in a high-throughput format, testing a panel of 12-15 antibiotics (including novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors like sulbactam-durlobactam, ceftazidime-avibactam, and legacy agents) against a curated collection of 50 genetically characterized CRE isolates. Synergy will be quantified using the Fractional Inhibitory Concentration Index (FICI) and Bliss independence models. Promising combinations will be validated in time-kill assays and analyzed against genotypic resistance profiles to identify mechanistic correlates.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a publicly accessible dataset of synergistic interactions for CRE, providing a roadmap for rational combination therapy. It will identify specific, high-potential combinations for further preclinical development and offer insights into resistance mechanisms that can be exploited therapeutically.
proposals.novelty: While synergy testing exists, this proposal systematically applies the high-throughput, multi-drug combinatorial screening paradigm from HIV to CRE, moving beyond standard two-drug testing to explore higher-order combinations and leveraging contemporary agents like sulbactam-durlobactam in novel pairings.

proposals.title: A Phase IIa, Randomized, Controlled Pilot Study Evaluating Low-Dose Meropenem-Vaborbactam for Prophylaxis Against Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections in Colonized, Immunocompromised Hematology Patients
proposals.motivation: Immunocompromised patients, particularly those with hematologic malignancies, are at high risk for life-threatening infections from multidrug-resistant organisms like CRE. Pre-emptive or prophylactic strategies are limited, and broad-spectrum antibiotics contribute to resistance. Meropenem-vaborbactam is a potent agent against KPC-producing CRE but has not been studied as a low-dose, targeted prophylaxis to prevent breakthrough infections in colonized patients, a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: A low-dose, prophylactic regimen of meropenem-vaborbactam will safely reduce the incidence of clinical CRE infections in immunocompromised patients with known gastrointestinal colonization, compared to standard infection control practices alone, without promoting breakthrough resistance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized, open-label pilot study. Adult hematology patients with CRE colonization (confirmed by rectal swab PCR and culture) and anticipated profound neutropenia will be randomized 1:1 to receive subcutaneous low-dose meropenem-vaborbactam (1g/1g every 12 hours) during neutropenic episodes plus standard care, or standard care alone. The primary endpoint is the incidence of microbiologically confirmed CRE infection within 30 days. Secondary endpoints include safety, all-cause mortality, and emergence of resistance via serial microbiologic and molecular analysis (e.g., whole-genome sequencing of isolates). Statistical analysis will use Fisher's exact test for the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical data on the feasibility and preliminary efficacy of a targeted, β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor-based prophylaxis for CRE. It will generate critical pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic and safety data for a novel low-dose regimen, informing future larger trials. The results could establish a new paradigm for precision infection prevention in high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., CACTUS) compares treatments for active MDR infections, this proposal shifts focus to pre-emptive prophylaxis using a scaled-down dose of a last-resort agent, a strategy not previously tested in clinical trials. It applies pharmacologic principles of suppression, rather than eradication, specifically in a defined, colonized cohort.

proposals.title: Population-Level Agent-Based Modeling of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Resistance Gene Dynamics in Response to Expanded Meropenem-Vaborbactam Use
proposals.motivation: Meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a critical agent for treating serious carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections, particularly those producing Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase (KPC). However, its increasing clinical adoption, driven by resistance to older agents like ceftazidime-avibactam, creates selective pressure that may alter the epidemiology of resistance genes. Pre-2026, there is a lack of predictive models to quantify how population-level MEV use influences the prevalence of specific β-lactamase genes (e.g., KPC variants, metallo-β-lactamases) and co-resistance patterns, hindering stewardship and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the widespread adoption of meropenem-vaborbactam will selectively reduce the prevalence of KPC-producing CRE but will concurrently drive an increase in the relative abundance of CRE harboring metallo-β-lactamases (e.g., NDM, VIM) and OXA-48-like enzymes, which are not inhibited by vaborbactam.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) simulating a population of patients and bacterial reservoirs. The model will incorporate real-world data on MEV prescription rates (from sources like Michigan's outpatient prescribing patterns), patient movement between hospital and community settings, and genomic surveillance data on CRE resistance gene distribution (informed by studies like CACTUS). The ABM will use a Susceptible-Infected-Resistant (SIR) framework with multiple resistance compartments. Key parameters will include MEV usage intensity, fitness costs of resistance genes, and horizontal gene transfer rates. The model will be calibrated using pre-2025 US surveillance data and validated against held-out datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, gene-centric forecast of CRE epidemiology under different MEV stewardship scenarios. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs and public health agencies by identifying usage thresholds that minimize the emergence of non-KPC CRE. The model framework will be adaptable for assessing other novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., CACTUS) compares drug efficacy in subgroups, this project moves beyond clinical outcomes to model the evolutionary dynamics of specific resistance genes at a population scale in response to a newer agent, MEV, integrating real-world prescribing data with genomic epidemiology in a dynamic transmission model.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the 12.5% Non-Inferiority Margin for Mortality in Severe Infection Trials: A Quantitative Analysis of Clinical Acceptability and Trial Feasibility
proposals.motivation: The 12.5% absolute risk difference margin for mortality in non-inferiority (NI) trials for severe infections, such as those caused by MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii, has become a de facto standard. This margin, while statistically conservative, may be overly stringent, leading to prohibitively large and expensive trials that delay the development of critically needed new antimicrobials like sulbactam-durlobactam. In the pre-2026 context of a stagnant antibiotic pipeline and rising antimicrobial resistance, reassessing the clinical appropriateness of this margin is urgent to improve trial feasibility without compromising patient safety.
proposals.hypothesis: A margin wider than 12.5% for all-cause mortality in NI trials for severe bacterial infections can be justified as clinically acceptable based on a quantitative synthesis of historical comparator drug performance, patient subgroup risk, and the net clinical benefit of new agents. We hypothesize that an evidence-based, variable margin (e.g., 15-20%) would maintain adequate assurance of efficacy while significantly increasing trial feasibility.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-database meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials and large observational cohorts (e.g., CACTUS study design) for severe Gram-negative infections treated with established comparator regimens (e.g., colistin-based therapy, ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam). Using Bayesian hierarchical models, we will estimate the distribution of absolute mortality risk with standard care across key patient subgroups. We will then employ a risk-benefit trade-off framework, incorporating pre-2026 data on secondary efficacy endpoints and toxicity, to model the maximum acceptable margin that preserves a positive net clinical benefit compared to the historical effect of the active control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative foundation for debating and potentially revising NI margins in severe infection trials. It will offer sponsors and regulators a data-driven methodology for determining context-specific margins, potentially reducing sample size requirements by 30-50%. The proposal directly supports antimicrobial development by addressing a major feasibility barrier highlighted in recent reviews of the antibiotic pipeline.
proposals.novelty: While the 12.5% margin is widely used, a comprehensive, infection-specific challenge integrating heterogeneous efficacy data (like that from subgroup analyses in CACTUS) with a formal net clinical benefit assessment has not been published. This moves beyond statistical arguments to a patient-centered, clinical outcome-based justification for margin selection.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Clinical Cure vs. 14-Day Mortality as Primary Endpoints in Trials for Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 antimicrobial trials for MDR infections, 14-day all-cause mortality is a common but potentially problematic primary endpoint. It may be insensitive to meaningful clinical improvement, especially in critically ill patients where death is influenced by numerous non-infectious factors, and may not reflect the true anti-infective effect of novel agents like sulbactam-durlobactam or ceftazidime-avibactam. Determining a more patient-centered, infection-specific endpoint is crucial for accurately assessing new therapies and supporting regulatory and clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: For trials evaluating antibiotics against MDR Gram-negative infections, a composite endpoint of clinical cure and microbiological eradication assessed at a fixed point (e.g., Test of Cure visit) is a more sensitive and specific measure of treatment efficacy and a stronger predictor of long-term patient outcomes than 14-day all-cause mortality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, observational cohort study using data from the CACTUS study and other relevant pre-2026 trial datasets (e.g., from Merck, Shionogi) focusing on MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii. We will apply multivariable logistic regression and Cox proportional hazards models to compare the association of 14-day mortality versus a composite clinical/microbiological cure endpoint with key long-term outcomes (e.g., 28-/30-day mortality, hospital readmission). We will perform sensitivity analyses on patient subgroups (e.g., immunocompromised, critically ill) to assess endpoint performance across populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence to guide endpoint selection in future antibiotic trials, potentially increasing their statistical power and clinical relevance. It will offer a framework for evaluating endpoints that can be applied by researchers and consultants designing studies. The findings will directly inform grant proposals and support discussions with regulatory bodies about acceptable primary outcomes for novel agents.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of mortality endpoints are acknowledged, a direct, data-driven comparison of their predictive validity against a rigorous composite cure endpoint, specifically within contemporary MDR infection cohorts and modern antibiotic regimens, has not been comprehensively performed. This analysis moves beyond theoretical discussion to provide actionable evidence for clinical trial design.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Empirical Spectrum of Cefiderocol: A Retrospective Cohort Study Including Patients with Solely Gram-Positive Culture Results
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a novel siderophore cephalosporin approved for treating Gram-negative infections. However, its in vitro activity against some Gram-positive pathogens, particularly Staphylococcus aureus, is documented but clinically underexplored. Standard efficacy trials exclude patients with only Gram-positive cultures, creating a knowledge gap regarding its potential empirical breadth and the real-world consequences of its use when Gram-negative infection is suspected but not confirmed.
proposals.hypothesis: Including patients with infections confirmed by solely Gram-positive cultures in an analysis of cefiderocol-treated cohorts will reveal a broader effective empirical spectrum than currently recognized in its label and will demonstrate non-inferior clinical outcomes compared to standard Gram-positive active therapies in these patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2019-2025. The cohort will include hospitalized patients who received cefiderocol for ≥72 hours for a suspected Gram-negative infection. We will create two analysis groups: 1) those with culture-confirmed Gram-negative infections (standard cohort) and 2) those with culture results identifying only Gram-positive pathogens (novel cohort). The primary outcome will be clinical cure at day 14. We will use propensity score matching to compare the novel cohort to a control group of similar patients with Gram-positive-only infections who received standard Gram-positive therapy (e.g., vancomycin). Subgroup analyses will focus on specific pathogens like MRSA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first real-world clinical evidence on the effectiveness of cefiderocol in patients with monomicrobial Gram-positive infections, directly informing its de facto empirical coverage. It will offer critical data for antimicrobial stewardship programs on the potential ecological impact and risk-benefit profile of using cefiderocol as a broad-spectrum empirical agent.
proposals.novelty: This analysis challenges the conventional exclusion criterion of Gram-positive-only cultures in studies of anti-Gram-negative agents. It leverages real-world prescribing data to test a drug's clinical activity beyond its approved indication and primary spectrum, a question not addressed in pivotal trials like CREDIBLE-CR or APEKS-NP.

proposals.title: Efficacy and Safety of Intravenous Cefiderocol Combined with Inhaled Amikacin for the Treatment of Carbapenem-Resistant Gram-Negative Pneumonia: A Phase IIb, Randomized, Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) Gram-negative pneumonia, particularly from pathogens like Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii, presents a critical therapeutic challenge with high mortality. While intravenous cefiderocol offers broad-spectrum activity, achieving optimal lung penetration remains a concern. Inhaled antibiotics deliver high local concentrations but may not adequately treat systemic infection or deep-seated lung foci, creating a rationale for combination therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Combining systemic cefiderocol with adjunctive inhaled amikacin will achieve superior clinical cure rates compared to cefiderocol monotherapy in patients with MDR Gram-negative pneumonia by enhancing bactericidal activity at the primary infection site while maintaining systemic coverage.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized, open-label, Phase IIb trial. Hospitalized adults with confirmed or suspected MDR Gram-negative pneumonia will be randomized 1:1 to receive either intravenous cefiderocol (2g q8h, 3-hour infusion) plus placebo nebulization, or intravenous cefiderocol plus inhaled amikacin (400mg BID via vibrating mesh nebulizer). The primary endpoint will be clinical cure at Test-of-Cure (Day 28). Key secondary endpoints include microbiological eradication, time to clinical stability, and safety assessments (renal function, ototoxicity). Pharmacokinetic analyses of serum and epithelial lining fluid (via bronchoalveolar lavage in a subset) will characterize lung penetration.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-quality clinical evidence on the efficacy and safety of this specific dual-route regimen for severe MDR pneumonia. It will generate critical pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data to inform dosing and establish a potential new standard-of-care framework for optimizing treatment outcomes in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While inhaled antibiotics are often used as salvage therapy, this study prospectively evaluates a rationally designed, first-line dual intravenous/inhaled regimen pairing a novel siderophore cephalosporin with an aminoglycoside, moving beyond the current paradigm of sequential or last-resort inhaled use.

proposals.title: A Comparative Effectiveness and Cost Analysis of Non-Antibiotic Primary Interventions for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The development of novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam) has been a primary focus for treating CRE, but resistance to these new agents is already emerging, and their high cost limits access. This creates an urgent need to evaluate non-antibiotic strategies as potential first-line interventions to preserve antimicrobial effectiveness and improve outcomes. Phage therapy and immunomodulation (e.g., with monoclonal antibodies or immune stimulants) represent promising but underexplored primary treatment paradigms in the pre-2026 clinical landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with CRE infections, initial treatment with a non-antibiotic primary strategy (phage therapy or adjunctive immunomodulation) will be associated with non-inferior clinical cure rates and lower total healthcare costs compared to initial treatment with newer, last-resort antimicrobial agents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from US hospitals (2018-2025). Adult patients with CRE bacteremia or pneumonia will be identified. Cohorts will be defined by initial treatment strategy: 1) newer antimicrobials (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam), 2) phage therapy (commercially available or investigational), or 3) standard antibiotic therapy plus an immunomodulatory agent (e.g., interferon-gamma, granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor). Primary outcomes will be 30-day all-cause mortality and total direct hospitalization costs. We will use propensity score matching and multivariable regression to adjust for confounders like severity of illness and source of infection, as seen in Pogue's prior CACTUS study methodology.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative effectiveness and economic data on non-antibiotic primary interventions for CRE, directly informing clinical decision-making and antimicrobial stewardship programs. It will shift the discourse from sequential antibiotic development to evaluating integrated, pathogen-specific and host-directed therapies as potential first-line options.
proposals.novelty: While Pogue's prior work compares newer antibiotics, this proposal directly challenges the paradigm that a new antibiotic must be the primary solution by positioning non-antibiotic modalities as initial, rather than salvage, therapy. It leverages real-world data to test a fundamentally different treatment strategy before large randomized trials are feasible.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy and Safety of Novel Pipeline Agents in a Pragmatic, Multi-Center Trial for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical public health threat with limited, often suboptimal treatment options and high mortality rates. While new agents like meropenem-vaborbactam and eravacycline have received regulatory approval based on pivotal trials, real-world comparative effectiveness data across diverse patient subgroups and infection sources are urgently needed to guide optimal clinical use and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a real-world population with serious CRE infections, treatment outcomes (clinical cure, microbiological eradication, and 30-day mortality) will differ significantly between novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations (e.g., meropenem-vaborbactam) and novel tetracycline derivatives (e.g., eravacycline), and that efficacy will be modified by key patient factors such as renal function, source of infection, and baseline severity of illness.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, multi-center, observational cohort study with a propensity-score matched analysis, leveraging the existing infrastructure of the CACTUS study. Adult inpatients with culture-confirmed CRE infections treated with a novel pipeline agent (meropenem-vaborbactam, eravacycline, or other pre-specified agents like ceftazidime-avibactam as an active comparator) will be enrolled. The primary endpoint is a composite of clinical cure and 30-day all-cause mortality. Secondary endpoints include microbiological eradication, hospital length of stay, and incidence of adverse events (e.g., nephrotoxicity, Clostridioides difficile infection). Subgroup analyses will mirror the CACTUS methodology, stratifying by infection source, APACHE-II score, and renal function.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate robust, real-world comparative effectiveness and safety data for the newest anti-CRE agents, directly informing treatment guidelines and stewardship programs. It will identify patient-specific factors predictive of success or failure with each agent class, enabling more personalized, effective therapy and potentially improving patient outcomes while preserving the utility of these last-line antibiotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies like the TANGO II trial established the efficacy of meropenem-vaborbactam versus best available therapy, this proposal directly compares multiple novel agents head-to-head in a real-world setting. It uniquely applies the patient-subgroup analytical framework from Dr. Pogue's CACTUS study on Pseudomonas to the distinct challenge of CRE, a gap in the current literature.

proposals.title: Pharmacometric Optimization of Fosfomycin and Tigecycline Dosing Regimens for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections: A Population Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic and Clinical Outcomes Analysis
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections represent a critical public health threat with limited, often suboptimal treatment options. Emerging agents like intravenous fosfomycin and tigecycline are used in salvage or combination therapy, but their dosing is not well-defined for severe CRE infections, leading to potential treatment failure from under-dosing or toxicity from over-exposure. Optimizing their use is essential to preserve efficacy and improve patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-informed precision dosing (MIPD) approach, integrating patient-specific factors and pathogen MICs, can identify optimized dosing regimens for fosfomycin and tigecycline that achieve superior pharmacodynamic target attainment against CRE compared to standard fixed dosing, thereby improving predicted clinical efficacy while minimizing toxicity risks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a population pharmacokinetic (PopPK) meta-analysis using pooled patient-level data from published clinical trials and therapeutic drug monitoring studies (pre-2026) for intravenous fosfomycin and tigecycline. Covariates (e.g., renal function, weight, severity of illness) will be tested for influence on PK parameters using nonlinear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). The resulting models will be used in Monte Carlo simulations (MCS) to calculate the probability of target attainment (PTA) for various dosing regimens against CRE MIC distributions. Toxicity thresholds (e.g., AUC for tigecycline liver effects) will be incorporated to define an optimal dosing window.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated PopPK/PD and toxicity assessment to define evidence-based, patient-stratified dosing recommendations for these agents against CRE. The proposal will deliver a practical dosing framework for clinicians and inform the design of future comparative effectiveness trials. It directly supports antimicrobial stewardship by promoting optimized use of last-resort agents.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined PK/PD of these drugs individually, this proposal uniquely applies a comparative, integrated safety-efficacy optimization framework specifically for the CRE population, leveraging real-world patient subgroup data as suggested by Dr. Pogue's prior work on subgroup impacts in MDR Pseudomonas.

proposals.title: Clinical and Microbiological Efficacy of Novel β-Lactam Combination Regimens for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE): A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical public health threat with limited, often toxic, treatment options. While newer agents like ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA) and meropenem-vaborbactam (MVB) are available, resistance to these drugs is emerging. There is a pressing clinical need, based on pre-2026 observational data and in vitro synergy studies, to evaluate whether combining these newer agents with older, potentially synergistic drugs (e.g., polymyxins, fosfomycin, aminoglycosides, or high-dose extended-infusion carbapenems) can improve outcomes in extensively drug-resistant (XDR) CRE infections.
proposals.hypothesis: For infections caused by XDR-CRE isolates with elevated minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) to newer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor agents, combination therapy regimens will be associated with improved clinical cure and reduced 30-day mortality compared to monotherapy with these newer agents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients treated for XDR-CRE infections from 2020-2025. The primary exposure will be combination therapy (a newer β-lactam agent plus ≥1 additional active in vitro agent) versus monotherapy. The primary outcome will be a composite of clinical cure and 30-day all-cause mortality. We will employ multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders, including source of infection, severity of illness (e.g., APACHE II), renal function, and specific resistance mechanisms (e.g., KPC, metallo-β-lactamase) when available from the electronic health record and linked microbiology data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence to guide antimicrobial stewardship for the most challenging CRE cases. It will directly inform treatment guidelines by quantifying the potential benefit of combination regimens in a high-risk population, potentially slowing resistance development by preserving newer agents through synergistic partnerships.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like the CACTUS study compared newer agents against each other, this proposal specifically investigates their use in *combination* for isolates with diminished susceptibility, a strategy suggested by in vitro data but lacking large-scale clinical validation. It leverages Dr. Pogue's expertise in patient subgroup analysis and antibiotic prescribing patterns to address a critical gap in MDR infection management.

proposals.title: A Consensus-Driven Framework for Standardized Endpoints to Evaluate Adjunctive Immunomodulators in Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia: A Comparative Trial Design
proposals.motivation: The evaluation of adjunctive immunomodulators for bacterial sepsis is hindered by heterogeneous primary outcome definitions across trials, preventing robust meta-analyses and direct comparisons between therapies. This heterogeneity, evident in recent trials for MDR pathogens like Pseudomonas aeruginosa, stalls progress in identifying optimal host-directed strategies for specific infections. A standardized, consensus-based endpoint framework is urgently needed to rationally accelerate this field.
proposals.hypothesis: A modified Delphi process involving key stakeholders (clinicians, researchers, regulators, and patient advocates) can achieve consensus on a standardized primary outcome measure for S. aureus bacteremia trials, and applying this measure will reveal significant efficacy differences between adjunctive tocilizumab and a JAK inhibitor (e.g., baricitinib) that were previously obscured by endpoint variability.
proposals.method: Phase 1: Conduct a structured, multi-round modified Delphi process with a panel of ~30 international experts to define and ratify a core outcome set (COS) for S. aureus bacteremia trials, prioritizing a composite endpoint (e.g., all-cause mortality, persistent bacteremia, relapse). Phase 2: Utilize this COS to design a pragmatic, multi-center comparative effectiveness trial. Patients with confirmed S. aureus bacteremia will be randomized to standard antibiotic therapy plus either tocilizumab, a JAK inhibitor, or placebo. The pre-specified, consensus-driven composite endpoint will serve as the primary outcome, with sample size calculated accordingly.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pathogen-specific, consensus-driven core outcome set for S. aureus bacteremia, directly enabling future trial harmonization. It will provide a head-to-head comparison of two distinct immunomodulatory strategies using a standardized metric, offering clear evidence to guide clinical practice. The methodology establishes a generalizable framework for developing similar COS for other high-priority infections.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond simply calling for standardization by actively creating a consensus endpoint and immediately deploying it in a direct comparative trial of immunomodulators, a design not yet executed in sepsis research. It integrates the Delphi method—common in guideline development—with a novel comparative efficacy study to solve a critical methodological roadblock.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Adaptive Platform Trial for Severe Infections: Simultaneously Evaluating Therapeutics and Refining Consensus Syndromic Definitions
proposals.motivation: Therapeutic trials for severe infections like sepsis and COVID-19 are hampered by heterogeneous patient populations and imprecise syndromic definitions, leading to failed trials and unclear generalizability. Concurrently, consensus definitions for 'complicated' vs. 'uncomplicated' infection syndromes (e.g., for Gram-negative bloodstream infections) are based on expert opinion but lack prospective validation for their prognostic and predictive utility. This creates a critical gap where treatment efficacy is measured in poorly defined cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating the prospective validation of syndromic definitions as an adaptive component within a therapeutic platform trial will create a synergistic feedback loop, where dynamically refined patient stratification improves the detection of treatment effects, and therapeutic response data validates the clinical relevance of the definitions.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-arm, Bayesian adaptive platform trial for severe infections, initially targeting COVID-19 and Gram-negative sepsis. The platform will test multiple immunomodulators or novel antimicrobials (e.g., leveraging expertise with agents like sulbactam-durlobactam). A core adaptive algorithm will use accumulating outcome data (e.g., 28-day mortality) to periodically update patient assignment probabilities and, crucially, to test the predictive value of different definitional criteria (e.g., SOFA score thresholds, organ failure markers). Using Bayesian model averaging, the trial will dynamically weight stratification rules based on their posterior probability of predicting treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for co-developing precise disease taxonomy and effective therapies, moving beyond static definitions. It will generate high-quality evidence on specific therapeutics while delivering empirically validated, dynamic syndromic definitions that can improve future trial design and clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive platform trials exist, none treat the syndromic definition itself as a core, dynamically optimized variable. This fusion of nosology refinement with therapeutic evaluation within a single adaptive engine is a novel methodological advance.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Adaptive Trial of Non-Operative Management for Presumed Uncomplicated Intra-Abdominal Infections: Validation of a Composite Long-Term Outcome Pathway
proposals.motivation: Current guidelines for infections like diverticulitis or cholecystitis often default to source control, but a significant subset of patients may be managed successfully with antibiotics alone, avoiding procedural risks. However, existing studies lack rigorous, patient-centered long-term outcomes, focusing instead on short-term clinical stability. This leads to potential over-treatment and fails to define which patients truly benefit from a non-operative approach.
proposals.hypothesis: A prospectively defined cohort of patients with presumed uncomplicated intra-abdominal infections, managed via a protocolized non-operative pathway with real-time safety monitoring, will achieve non-inferiority compared to standard immediate source control, as measured by a composite endpoint of 90-day mortality, infection recurrence, unplanned source control, and patient-reported functional recovery.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, prospective, adaptive non-inferiority trial. Eligible patients (e.g., with imaging-confirmed, localized diverticulitis or acute cholecystitis meeting strict clinical criteria) will be enrolled in the non-operative pathway (NOM). The primary endpoint is a composite of 90-day all-cause mortality, infection recurrence, need for unplanned source control, and failure to return to baseline on the PROMIS Global Health scale. An embedded, Bayesian safety monitoring algorithm will analyze pre-specified interim outcomes (e.g., clinical deterioration, persistent fever) in real-time. If the posterior probability of harm exceeds a pre-set threshold, the trial will adapt by pausing enrollment for that patient subgroup, triggering a protocol-defined escalation to source control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-level evidence for a standardized, safe NOM pathway using a clinically meaningful, long-term composite endpoint. It will generate a validated clinical decision rule to identify patients most suitable for NOM, directly informing future guidelines. The study also serves as a model for ethically evaluating de-escalation strategies in infectious diseases using adaptive designs with embedded safety.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the rigorous, patient-centered composite endpoint that moves beyond 30-day mortality or clinical cure, and the integration of a real-time Bayesian safety monitor within a pragmatic trial design to ensure ethical conduct while testing a de-escalation strategy.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled, Non-Inferiority Trial Comparing 5-Day versus 7-Day Antibiotic Courses for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections (GNBSI)
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship efforts aim to reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure to curb resistance and adverse events. While the shift from 14 to 7 days for uncomplicated GNBSI is established, the optimal minimum effective duration remains undefined. Further shortening therapy could significantly reduce antibiotic days, aligning with stewardship goals and potentially improving patient safety without compromising efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: A 5-day course of appropriate antibiotic therapy is non-inferior to a 7-day course for the treatment of uncomplicated GNBSI, as measured by the composite endpoint of clinical cure and microbiological eradication at a 30-day test-of-cure visit.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized, double-blind, controlled non-inferiority trial. Adult patients with uncomplicated GNBSI (e.g., source-controlled, afebrile, hemodynamically stable) caused by Enterobacterales or non-MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa will be randomized to receive 5 or 7 days of active antibiotic therapy (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, or a carbapenem, based on susceptibility). The primary endpoint is a composite of clinical cure and microbiological eradication at day 30 post-randomization. A non-inferiority margin of 10% will be used. Secondary endpoints include 90-day recurrence, new resistance emergence, and Clostridioides difficile infection rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to guide definitive duration recommendations for uncomplicated GNBSI, potentially establishing a new standard of care. It directly contributes to antimicrobial stewardship by testing a strategy to safely reduce antibiotic use. The findings will inform clinical guidelines and offer a practical intervention to mitigate selection pressure for resistance.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have validated 7-day courses, no large-scale randomized trial has prospectively tested a 5-day regimen for GNBSI. This proposal moves beyond the established 7-day rule to explore the true lower bound of effective therapy, a critical next step in stewardship not yet addressed in the literature.

proposals.title: Defining Uncomplicated Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections: A Novel Clinical Decision Framework Based on Ecological Succession Principles
proposals.motivation: Current clinical practice often mandates aggressive, broad-spectrum therapy for all infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens like Pseudomonas aeruginosa, driven by a paradigm of universal threat. This contributes to antibiotic overuse, collateral damage to the microbiome, and increased healthcare costs. An ecological 'novel ecosystems' framework, which moved beyond blanket eradication of non-native species to assess functional integration, offers a novel lens to challenge this one-size-fits-all approach in infectious diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: A subset of MDR P. aeruginosa infections, characterized by specific host, pathogen, and infection-site parameters, can be defined as 'uncomplicated' and managed effectively with streamlined, targeted antimicrobial therapy without compromising patient outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients with MDR P. aeruginosa infections (e.g., from the CACTUS study database and local EHRs). Using a multivariable logistic regression model, we will identify independent predictors of clinical success with narrower-spectrum or shorter-duration regimens. Key parameters will include quantitative culture burden (<10^4 CFU/mL), absence of high-risk virulence genes (e.g., exoU) via available PCR assays, host inflammatory markers (e.g., procalcitonin trend), and non-critical infection site (e.g., uncomplicated cystitis). These will be synthesized into a point-based clinical decision algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based framework for de-escalating therapy in select MDR Gram-negative infections, directly supporting antimicrobial stewardship goals. It aims to reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure, lower treatment costs, and establish a new paradigm for pathogen management that balances efficacy with ecological impact.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in directly applying ecological theory to clinical infectious disease decision-making and in operationally defining 'uncomplicated' for traditionally feared MDR pathogens, a concept not formally established in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of De-Escalated Management for Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Bacteremia: A Decision-Tree and Markov Model
proposals.motivation: Current IDSA guidelines for managing MDR Gram-negative bacteremias, especially those involving pathogens like Pseudomonas aeruginosa, mandate prolonged therapy and routine follow-up blood cultures, driving significant healthcare costs and resource use. However, emerging clinical data, such as from the CACTUS study, suggests patient subgroups may have comparable outcomes with less intensive regimens. An economic evaluation is needed to determine if a selective, de-escalated pathway could be a cost-effective alternative to standard care.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific patient subgroups with MDR P. aeruginosa bacteremia (e.g., those with achieved source control and low severity of illness), a de-escalated management strategy using shorter, uncomplicated antibiotic courses without mandatory repeat blood cultures will be cost-effective compared to standard guideline-recommended management.
