proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid decision-tree and Markov state-transition model from a US healthcare payer perspective. The model will compare a 'De-escalated Pathway' (e.g., 7-day therapy, no repeat cultures if clinically improving) against 'Standard Guideline Care'. Clinical inputs (failure, relapse, mortality rates) will be derived from recent subgroup analyses (e.g., CACTUS) and meta-analyses of pre-2026 literature. Cost inputs will be obtained from Medicare reimbursement schedules and published hospital cost data. We will perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses, with value-of-information analysis to identify key research gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated economic model challenging the intensive management paradigm for MDR pathogens. It will quantify the potential cost savings and value of a tailored approach, offering an evidence-based economic argument to inform future guideline revisions and antimicrobial stewardship policies in high-resistance settings.
proposals.novelty: This analysis moves beyond clinical efficacy to formally model the economic implications of selectively applying de-escalation principles to infections traditionally excluded from such strategies, directly addressing a gap in the cost-effectiveness literature for MDR Gram-negative infections.

proposals.title: Defining Uncomplicated Infection Phenotypes: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Oral Step-Down Therapy for Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterobacterales Infections
proposals.motivation: Current guidelines universally classify infections involving Pseudomonas aeruginosa or resistant Enterobacterales as complicated, mandating prolonged intravenous therapy. This paradigm, based on historical treatment failures, may not reflect the efficacy of modern oral step-down agents like fluoroquinolones or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for susceptible isolates. Overly conservative definitions contribute to unnecessary hospital stays, increased healthcare costs, and patient burden.
proposals.hypothesis: A distinct, low-risk clinical phenotype exists among patients with infections caused by these pathogens, characterized by early clinical response, source control, and susceptible oral antibiotic options, for whom transition to oral therapy is non-inferior to continued IV therapy in terms of clinical cure and relapse.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024. Adults with bloodstream or urinary tract infections due to MDR P. aeruginosa or Enterobacterales (ESBL, CRE) will be identified. Using propensity score matching, we will compare outcomes (30-day clinical cure, 90-day relapse, mortality) between patients who received early oral step-down (≤7 days IV) versus prolonged IV therapy (>7 days). A machine learning algorithm (random forest) will be applied to identify key predictors (e.g., Pitt bacteremia score, source control, oral agent MIC) of success with oral step-down, defining a potential 'uncomplicated' phenotype.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence to challenge the universal 'complicated' classification for resistant gram-negative infections. It will generate a data-driven, risk-stratification tool to identify candidates for early oral transition, directly informing future stewardship guidelines and potentially reducing length of stay. The findings will offer a pragmatic framework for antimicrobial de-escalation in the MDR era.
proposals.novelty: While recent trials (e.g., CACTUS) compare IV agents, they do not question the fundamental classification of the infection itself. This proposal directly tests the core assumption that pathogen identity alone dictates complexity, shifting focus to host and treatment-response factors to define management strategy.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Approach to Define Post-Discharge Superinfection Phenotypes in Patients Treated with Tocilizumab: A Real-World Data Study
proposals.motivation: Immunomodulatory agents like tocilizumab are critical for managing conditions like severe COVID-19 and rheumatoid arthritis, but they carry a known risk of serious infection. Current post-market surveillance and clinical studies rely on pre-defined, often short-term endpoints, lacking robust methods to identify complex, delayed infectious complications that manifest weeks after hospital discharge. This gap limits our understanding of the long-term safety profile of these therapies in diverse, real-world populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models can identify distinct, data-driven clinical phenotypes associated with increased risk of post-discharge Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) and other superinfections in patients treated with tocilizumab, using longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) data without relying on expert-consensus outcome definitions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using EHR data from a large U.S. health system. Patients receiving tocilizumab during an index hospitalization will be followed for 90 days post-discharge. We will engineer features from structured EHR data (demographics, lab values, prior antibiotic use, comorbidities) and temporal sequences of clinical events. A two-stage modeling approach will be used: first, an unsupervised method (e.g., latent class analysis or trajectory clustering) to identify potential risk phenotypes. Second, supervised models (e.g., gradient boosting machines like XGBoost or temporal convolutional networks) will be trained to predict the phenotype of superinfection risk, with validation against adjudicated infection outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a scalable, data-driven framework for defining and detecting long-term infectious risk phenotypes, moving beyond static definitions. It will provide a novel tool for pharmacovigilance and post-market safety surveillance of immunomodulators. The resulting model could help stratify patients for targeted monitoring or antimicrobial stewardship interventions after discharge.
proposals.novelty: This approach innovates by using unsupervised and supervised ML to *define* the outcome phenotype from data, rather than applying a pre-specified definition, for long-term post-discharge infections. It specifically focuses on the understudied period following immunomodulator administration, creating a model for continuous safety assessment.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Oral Step-down Antibiotic Regimens for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A Multi-center Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a major cause of morbidity and mortality. While oral step-down therapy is common for uncomplicated cases, there is limited real-world evidence comparing the clinical outcomes of different oral regimens, such as fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and oral beta-lactams. Optimizing this selection is critical to improve patient outcomes, minimize adverse effects, and combat antibiotic resistance, aligning with antimicrobial stewardship priorities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for patients with uncomplicated GNBSI transitioning from intravenous therapy, oral fluoroquinolone step-down will be associated with a lower rate of 30-day clinical failure (composite of recurrence, re-admission, or death) compared to other oral antibiotic classes, after adjusting for pathogen susceptibility and patient comorbidities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024. Adult patients with uncomplicated GNBSI (E. coli, Klebsiella spp., Pseudomonas aeruginosa) who received ≥48 hours of IV therapy followed by oral step-down will be included. The primary outcome is 30-day clinical failure. Using multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching, we will compare outcomes between oral antibiotic classes (fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, oral beta-lactams). Key covariates will include source control, minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values, renal function, and comorbidities. Subgroup analyses will focus on Pseudomonas aeruginosa and ESBL-producing Enterobacterales.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world comparative effectiveness data to guide oral antibiotic selection for GNBSI, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and treatment guidelines. It will identify patient and pathogen-specific factors associated with oral regimen success, helping to personalize therapy and potentially reduce unnecessary fluoroquinolone use.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like the CACTUS study compared IV agents for resistant Pseudomonas, this study uniquely focuses on the understudied oral step-down phase across a broad GNBSI population, integrating detailed susceptibility data (MICs) and modern comparative effectiveness methods to address a critical clinical decision point.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Procalcitonin-Guided De-escalation of Tocilizumab in Severe COVID-19 Patients at Risk for Bacterial Co-infection
proposals.motivation: IL-6 receptor antagonists like tocilizumab are standard of care for severe COVID-19, but their immunosuppressive effects may increase the risk of secondary bacterial infections, a leading cause of mortality. Universal, fixed-duration therapy in all patients may lead to unnecessary drug exposure, adverse events, and excess cost. An economic evaluation of a biomarker-driven strategy to personalize this costly therapy is urgently needed to inform stewardship and resource allocation in healthcare systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A de-escalation protocol for tocilizumab, triggered by low or declining procalcitonin levels to indicate low bacterial co-infection risk, will be cost-effective compared to universal therapy by reducing drug-related adverse events and unnecessary antibiotic use without compromising clinical efficacy.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision-analytic Markov model comparing a biomarker-guided strategy to standard care. The model will simulate a cohort of hospitalized adults with severe COVID-19, incorporating probabilities of clinical deterioration, secondary infection, and mortality derived from recent trials (e.g., RECOVERY, REMAP-CAP) and real-world data (e.g., CACTUS study insights). Costs (drugs, diagnostics, hospitalization) and utilities (QALYs) will be sourced from pre-2026 literature and U.S. databases. We will perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses to identify key cost drivers and model robustness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first formal economic framework to guide IL-6 antagonist stewardship in COVID-19, directly linking diagnostic biomarker use to pharmacoeconomic outcomes. It will offer hospital administrators and policymakers a quantitative rationale for implementing personalized de-escalation protocols. The findings will also contribute methodologically to the economic evaluation of biomarker-guided therapy in critical care.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have assessed the clinical safety of IL-6 blockade or the prognostic value of biomarkers like procalcitonin separately, this analysis uniquely integrates these clinical questions into a health economic model to evaluate a specific, actionable de-escalation protocol for a high-cost therapy.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model to Simulate the Hospital-Wide Impact of Consensus Guidelines on Ceftazidime-Avibactam and Ceftolozane-Tazobactam Use Against Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa
proposals.motivation: The rise of multidrug-resistant (MDR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa necessitates the strategic use of newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations like ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA) and ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T). However, real-world adoption of consensus guidelines is slow, and the population-level consequences of prescribing shifts on resistance patterns and patient outcomes are difficult to predict from clinical trials or retrospective studies alone. A dynamic, systems-level approach is needed to inform stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent-based model (ABM) calibrated with real-world patient subgroup efficacy data will demonstrate that hospital-wide adoption of consensus guidelines for CZA and C/T use will significantly reduce the incidence of treatment failure and delay the emergence of resistance compared to observed prescribing patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop an ABM using the NetLogo platform, representing a simulated hospital with agents for patients, physicians, and microbiological environments. Patient agents will be assigned attributes (e.g., infection source, prior resistance) based on distributions from the CACTUS study data. Physician agents will follow prescribing rules derived from either observed patterns (control) or consensus guideline algorithms (intervention). The model will incorporate pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic principles for antibiotic effect and stochastic rules for resistance gene transfer and selection, validated against historical unit-specific resistance data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic tool for antimicrobial stewardship programs to visualize and quantify the long-term benefits of guideline adherence. It will move the field beyond static recommendations by offering a simulated evidence base for the population-level impact of prescribing decisions on resistance and clinical outcomes, directly supporting grant applications for implementation studies.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs have been used for general antibiotic resistance, this model is novel in its specific integration of comparative effectiveness data from key patient subgroups (CACTUS) and its direct linkage to actionable, drug-specific consensus guidelines for MDR Pseudomonas, creating a tailored decision-support tool.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework Integrating Genomic and Clinical Data to Predict Minocycline Susceptibility and Treatment Failure Risk in Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections
proposals.motivation: Minocycline, particularly in its intravenous formulation, has re-emerged as a critical therapeutic option for multidrug-resistant (MDR) Acinetobacter baumannii and other difficult-to-treat Gram-negative pathogens. However, susceptibility testing is not universally performed, and treatment failure remains a significant risk. Pre-2026, predictive models for antibiotic resistance largely focus on beta-lactams; a dedicated model for minocycline, integrating both genomic markers and clinical risk factors, is lacking but urgently needed to guide precision therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on bacterial whole-genome sequencing data and patient-level clinical variables can accurately predict minocycline minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and the risk of clinical treatment failure, outperforming models based on single data modalities.
proposals.method: We will curate a retrospective cohort of patients with MDR Gram-negative infections treated with minocycline, with linked isolate genomes and clinical data (e.g., source of infection, prior antibiotic exposure, comorbidities). Feature engineering will include known resistance gene presence (e.g., tet genes, efflux pumps), single nucleotide polymorphisms, and clinical variables. We will train and compare multiple algorithms, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost, LightGBM) and neural networks, using nested cross-validation to predict binary susceptibility (S/I/R) and a composite outcome of treatment failure. Model interpretability will be assessed using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, interpretable clinical decision support tool to predict minocycline efficacy before susceptibility results are available. It will identify novel genetic determinants of minocycline resistance and quantify the contribution of clinical risk factors to treatment failure, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and personalized treatment regimens.
proposals.novelty: While ML for resistance prediction exists, this proposal is novel in its specific focus on minocycline—a drug of growing importance in the MDR era—and its explicit integration of genomic predictors with real-world clinical variables to forecast patient-level outcomes, not just in vitro MIC.

proposals.title: Population-Level Impact Modeling of Intravenous Minocycline for Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections on Hospital-Wide Resistance and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The introduction of new antibiotics like ceftazidime-avibactam and sulbactam-durlobactam has created complex stewardship challenges. Optimizing their use while preserving activity requires strategic deployment of older agents like intravenous minocycline for infections caused by multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii and Stenotrophomonas maltophilia. Pre-2026, the hospital-wide ecological impact of scaling up minocycline use on overall Gram-negative resistance patterns and patient outcomes remains poorly quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strategic, guideline-driven introduction of intravenous minocycline for targeted multidrug-resistant Gram-negative infections will be associated with a reduction in hospital-wide use of broader-spectrum beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations, without a concomitant increase in minocycline resistance or negative impacts on patient mortality.
proposals.method: We will construct a compartmental transmission model (Susceptible-Infected-Colonized) parameterized with real-world data from a multi-hospital electronic health record database (e.g., Merative MarketScan). The model will incorporate patient movement between wards, antibiotic exposure (minocycline, ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, carbapenems), and resistance gene dynamics for tet(X) and common beta-lactamases. We will use an agent-based simulation framework (e.g., AnyLogic) to project resistance prevalence and defined daily doses (DDD) over a 36-month period under different minocycline adoption scenarios, comparing outcomes to a counterfactual 'no-minocycline' baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, dynamic model to forecast the stewardship impact of reintroducing an older antibiotic at scale. It will offer hospital epidemiologists and antimicrobial stewardship teams a data-driven tool for predicting ecological consequences of formulary changes, moving beyond single-agent efficacy to population-level effect.
proposals.novelty: While prior work, including Dr. Pogue's CACTUS study, compares clinical efficacy of newer agents, this model uniquely integrates pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data for minocycline with hospital-wide transmission dynamics to project the indirect, ecological benefits of its use on preserving newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors.

proposals.title: Comparative Virulence and Resistance in Carbapenem-Resistant vs. Carbapenem-Susceptible Pseudomonas aeruginosa: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Determine Primary Drivers of Mortality
proposals.motivation: The excess mortality associated with carbapenem-resistant (CR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections is often attributed to treatment limitations due to resistance. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests that CR strains may harbor distinct virulence factors that independently worsen outcomes. Disentangling the contributions of resistance from virulence is critical for developing targeted interventions, such as anti-virulence therapies or novel antimicrobials like sulbactam-durlobactam, to improve patient survival.
proposals.hypothesis: The increased mortality observed in CR P. aeruginosa infections, compared to carbapenem-susceptible (CS) infections, is primarily driven by a higher prevalence and expression of specific virulence factors (e.g., exotoxins, secretion systems) in CR strains, rather than by antimicrobial resistance alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients with P. aeruginosa bloodstream infections (2018-2024). Clinical isolates will be sequenced to determine resistance genotype (e.g., blaKPC, blaNDM) and the presence of key virulence genes (e.g., exoU, exoS, type III secretion system). A multivariable Cox proportional hazards model will be used to assess the independent association of virulence genotype and resistance phenotype with 30-day all-cause mortality, adjusting for key confounders (e.g., APACHE-II, source control, appropriate empiric therapy). A mediation analysis will quantify the proportion of the mortality difference explained by virulence versus resistance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative evidence partitioning mortality risk between virulence and resistance in CR P. aeruginosa. It will inform the design of future clinical trials for pathogen-specific therapies and guide antimicrobial stewardship by clarifying when aggressive source control or adjunctive therapies may be most critical. The findings will directly support grant applications for novel treatment strategies beyond traditional antibiotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies like CACTUS compare outcomes between antibiotic regimens, this proposal directly tests the pathogenic mechanism behind the outcome disparity itself. It moves beyond correlative epidemiology by integrating whole-genome sequencing for virulence profiling with advanced statistical mediation analysis, a method not routinely applied in this clinical context pre-2026.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity of Clinical Outcomes within Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections: A Subgroup Analysis Based on MIC Distribution and Resistance Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: In clinical trials and observational studies, carbapenem-resistant (CR) and carbapenem-susceptible (CS) Pseudomonas aeruginosa are treated as homogeneous groups for outcome comparisons. However, CR isolates exhibit a wide range of minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) and diverse underlying resistance mechanisms (e.g., metallo-beta-lactamases, efflux pumps, porin loss). This broad categorization may mask significant differences in patient outcomes within the CR group, potentially obscuring the true efficacy of newer agents like ceftazidime-avibactam and ceftolozane-tazobactam against specific resistance phenotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within infections caused by carbapenem-resistant P. aeruginosa, clinical outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, clinical cure) will significantly differ based on the carbapenem MIC and the presence of specific resistance genes, indicating that the 'CR' label represents a heterogeneous population with distinct prognostic subgroups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from the CACTUS study and other pre-2026 MDR P. aeruginosa datasets. Isolates categorized as CR will be stratified by meropenem or imipenem MIC (e.g., 4-8 µg/mL vs. ≥16 µg/mL) and by resistance mechanism via available genotypic data. The primary outcome will be 30-day all-cause mortality. We will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for key confounders (e.g., APACHE-II, source of infection, renal function), to assess the independent association of MIC stratum and resistance mechanism with outcomes. A secondary analysis will evaluate treatment effects within these newly defined subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence that the binary CR/CS classification is insufficient for predicting patient risk or evaluating novel antibiotic performance. It will propose a more nuanced, mechanism- and MIC-based framework for stratifying CR P. aeruginosa infections in clinical research. The findings could inform the design of future antimicrobial trials and guide more personalized treatment approaches for MDR infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares outcomes between CR and CS groups, this analysis directly challenges the internal homogeneity of the CR category itself—a critical but often overlooked assumption. It integrates phenotypic (MIC) and genotypic data to define prognostically relevant subgroups, moving beyond a simple susceptible/resistant dichotomy.

proposals.title: Linking Genomic Epidemiology and Clinical Burden: A Comparative Analysis of Carbapenem-Resistant versus Carbapenem-Susceptible Pseudomonas aeruginosa in a U.S. Healthcare Network
proposals.motivation: The clinical and economic burden of carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) is severe, but the relative contribution of specific resistance mechanisms and high-risk clones to patient outcomes and healthcare costs remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026, studies like CACTUS compare antibiotic efficacy in subgroups, yet a comprehensive, strain-resolved analysis linking genomic data to detailed clinical and economic metrics is lacking. This gap hinders targeted infection control and stewardship efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the increased clinical burden associated with CRPA, compared to carbapenem-susceptible P. aeruginosa (CSPA), is driven not merely by resistance phenotype but by the convergence of specific high-risk sequence types (STs) and defined resistance determinants (e.g., metallo-beta-lactamases, efflux pump overexpression) which are independently associated with worse clinical outcomes and higher resource utilization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with P. aeruginosa infections from 2020-2024 within a multi-hospital network. Isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing. Bioinformatic analysis will use the Nullarbor pipeline (v2.0) for assembly, MLST typing, and resistance gene detection via ABRicate against ResFinder and CARD databases. Clinical data (demographics, outcomes, costs) will be extracted from electronic records. Statistical analysis will employ multivariable regression and machine learning (e.g., random forest models) to identify genomic predictors of 30-day mortality, length of stay, and direct treatment costs, comparing CRPA and CSPA strains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed molecular epidemiological map of CRPA in a U.S. setting, directly linking strain types and resistance mechanisms to quantifiable clinical and economic burdens. It will identify the highest-priority clones for intervention and inform the design of rapid diagnostic and stewardship strategies, such as those relevant to newer agents like ceftazidime-avibactam and sulbactam-durlobactam.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterizes resistance mechanisms or compares antibiotic efficacy, this study integrates detailed genomic, clinical outcome, and granular economic data into a single analytical framework to dissect the drivers of burden, moving beyond phenotype to define the most consequential strain-resistance combinations.

proposals.title: Integrating a Patient-Subgroup Burden Methodology with Global AMR Surveillance: A Framework for Comparative Analysis of Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) surveillance networks like GLASS and the US CDC's National Healthcare Safety Network (NHSN) collect vital data but often lack standardized methodologies to quantify the differential clinical burden of resistant infections across patient subgroups. This gap limits the ability to compare the real-world impact of pathogens like MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa and the effectiveness of newer agents like ceftazidime-avibactam and ceftolozane-tazobactam across healthcare systems and nations. A scalable, standardized burden methodology is needed to inform targeted interventions and stewardship policies globally.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a patient-subgroup burden methodology, developed from US-based studies like CACTUS, with international AMR surveillance data will reveal significant, previously unquantified variations in the clinical and economic impact of MDR Gram-negative infections across different healthcare settings and geographic regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the patient-subgroup analysis framework from the CACTUS study to create a standardized burden calculation module. This module will use inputs such as infection type, pathogen susceptibility, patient comorbidities (e.g., from ICD codes), and treatment outcomes. We will design an interoperability pipeline to map and harmonize data from the US NHSN and the WHO GLASS database using common data models (e.g., OMOP CDM) and specific algorithms for resistance phenotype classification and patient stratification. The analysis will employ multivariate regression models to compare burden metrics (e.g., attributable length of stay, mortality, treatment failure) across pre-defined international regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-access framework for calculating and comparing the burden of AMR across surveillance networks, moving beyond incidence and prevalence to patient-centered outcomes. It will provide the first multi-national, comparative analysis of the burden associated with MDR Pseudomonas aeruginosa, identifying high-risk patient populations and regions with the greatest need for intervention. The framework will directly support global antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention efforts.
proposals.novelty: While surveillance networks exist, the integration of a patient-stratified burden methodology for comparative international analysis is novel. This proposal moves beyond descriptive epidemiology to apply a pharmacoepidemiologic lens, used in Pogue's prior work on subgroup efficacy, to global surveillance data, creating a new tool for priority-setting.

proposals.title: Clinical and Healthcare Utilization Characteristics of Survivors with Carbapenem-Resistant Acinetobacter baumannii Infections and Short Hospital Length of Stay: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Research on carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) infections predominantly focuses on high-mortality, high-burden cases, creating a deficit in understanding patients who survive with lower healthcare utilization. Identifying characteristics and care pathways of these 'low-burden survivors' is crucial for defining optimal, resource-efficient management strategies and for informing realistic benchmarks in antimicrobial stewardship and value-based care models.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with CRAB infections who survive with a short length of stay (LOS) represent a distinct clinical phenotype characterized by earlier appropriate antibiotic therapy, lower severity of illness scores at presentation, and specific infection sources (e.g., urinary tract) compared to patients with prolonged LOS or who die.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from US hospitals (2019-2025). Adult inpatients with a positive CRAB culture and subsequent discharge alive will be included. The primary outcome is short LOS, defined as ≤ 75th percentile for survivors. We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify independent predictors of short LOS, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities (Charlson Comorbidity Index), infection source, severity (e.g., SOFA score), and antibiotic therapy timing/choice (including agents like sulbactam-durlobactam, ceftazidime-avibactam, and ceftolozane-tazobactam). A classification and regression tree (CART) analysis will be employed to define high-probability clinical profiles for short LOS.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first dedicated characterization of the 'low-burden survivor' population in CRAB infections, identifying modifiable factors in the care pathway associated with efficient resolution. The findings will help clinicians prospectively identify patients who may be candidates for shorter therapy or earlier transition to outpatient care, directly supporting antimicrobial stewardship and efforts to reduce hospital-acquired complications and costs.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical paradigm of studying poor outcomes in CRAB to instead investigate factors associated with a *good* and *efficient* outcome, a perspective largely absent from the pre-2026 literature. It leverages real-world prescribing pattern expertise to dissect the antibiotic management component of this efficient care pathway.

proposals.title: Long-term Survival and Resistance Emergence in Patients Treated with Ceftolozane-Tazobactam for Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T) is a critical agent for treating multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and other Gram-negative infections. While pivotal trials established short-term efficacy, real-world data on long-term patient outcomes (e.g., 90-day and 1-year survival) and the risk of subsequent resistance development are limited. Understanding these long-term consequences is essential for antimicrobial stewardship, guiding treatment duration, and informing the sequence of novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while C/T treatment is associated with favorable short-term clinical cure, a significant proportion of survivors will experience poor long-term survival attributable to underlying comorbidities and infection sequelae, and that treatment-emergent resistance to C/T and cross-resistance to other novel beta-lactams (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam) will be observed in a clinically relevant minority of cases.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients who received ≥72 hours of C/T for a confirmed Gram-negative infection between 2015-2024. Data on demographics, microbiology, treatment, and outcomes will be extracted from electronic health records (EHR) via a standardized REDCap form. The primary outcome is all-cause mortality at 90 days and 1 year post-treatment initiation, analyzed using Kaplan-Meier survival curves and Cox proportional hazards models adjusted for severity (e.g., Pitt bacteremia score, comorbidities). Secondary outcomes include the incidence of subsequent infections with C/T-non-susceptible or cross-resistant Gram-negative isolates, characterized by broth microdilution testing and whole-genome sequencing of paired isolates to identify resistance mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence quantifying long-term survival after C/T therapy, moving beyond discharge outcomes. It will directly estimate the risk of treatment-emergent resistance and cross-resistance, informing clinical guidelines on post-treatment monitoring and the strategic use of subsequent antibiotic lines. The findings will support antimicrobial stewardship programs in optimizing the use of last-resort agents.
proposals.novelty: This work extends beyond existing comparative efficacy studies (like CACTUS) by focusing on longitudinal outcomes and resistance development specifically linked to C/T exposure, integrating long-term survival analysis with advanced microbiological characterization of resistance mechanisms in a real-world cohort.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Ceftolozane-Tazobactam versus Polymyxin- or Aminoglycoside-Based Regimens for Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections in the United States
proposals.motivation: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections are associated with high mortality, prolonged hospital stays, and significant healthcare costs. While newer agents like ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T) demonstrate superior efficacy and safety compared to older, more toxic regimens like polymyxins and aminoglycosides, their higher acquisition cost presents a barrier to use. A formal health economic analysis is urgently needed to inform hospital formulary decisions and antimicrobial stewardship policies in the pre-2026 era of constrained budgets and rising resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that despite its higher drug acquisition cost, C/T will be cost-effective compared to polymyxin- or aminoglycoside-based regimens for MDR P. aeruginosa infections due to reductions in mortality, nephrotoxicity-related complications, and length of hospital stay.
proposals.method: We will construct a decision-analytic Markov model from the US healthcare sector perspective, simulating a cohort of hospitalized patients with MDR P. aeruginosa infections. Clinical inputs for efficacy, mortality, and nephrotoxicity rates will be derived from recent comparative studies, including the CACTUS trial and real-world evidence. Cost data will be obtained from hospital billing databases, Medicare reimbursement schedules, and published literature. We will calculate incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) in dollars per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gained, employing deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses to assess uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive US-based cost-effectiveness model directly comparing C/T to legacy polymyxin/aminoglycoside-based regimens. It will deliver actionable evidence for hospital pharmacists, infectious disease consultants, and stewardship teams to justify the value of newer beta-lactam agents. The findings will support more informed resource allocation and guideline development for managing MDR Gram-negative infections.
proposals.novelty: While clinical efficacy of C/T is established, its economic value versus the specific, high-toxicity comparator of polymyxin/aminoglycoside regimens has not been rigorously modeled from a US perspective, integrating recent real-world evidence on patient subgroups and outcomes.

proposals.title: Impact of Rapid Molecular Diagnostic Implementation on Time to Appropriate Ceftolozane-Tazobactam Therapy and Clinical Outcomes in Patients with Suspected Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections
proposals.motivation: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections are associated with high mortality, and delays in effective therapy worsen outcomes. Ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T) is a critical agent for these infections, but its optimal use is hindered by the 24-48 hour turnaround time of conventional culture and susceptibility testing. Rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) like multiplex PCR panels can identify pathogens and resistance markers from positive blood cultures in hours, yet their impact on guiding newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors like C/T in real-world settings is not fully characterized.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a diagnostic stewardship protocol using a rapid molecular diagnostic test for Gram-negative bacteremia will significantly reduce the time to appropriate C/T therapy and improve clinical outcomes compared to standard microbiology workflows.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, pre-post study at a large academic medical center. The intervention is the implementation of a stewardship protocol where a multiplex PCR panel (e.g., BioFire Blood Culture Identification 2 panel) is performed on all positive blood cultures with Gram-negative rods, with results reviewed immediately by an antimicrobial stewardship pharmacist (PharmD). For patients with suspected MDR P. aeruginosa (e.g., prior resistance, healthcare exposure), the protocol will recommend early C/T initiation if the RDT detects P. aeruginosa and relevant beta-lactamase resistance genes (e.g., blaCTX-M). The primary outcome is time from positive blood culture to appropriate C/T administration. Secondary outcomes include 30-day mortality, hospital length of stay, and C/T days of therapy. Data will be analyzed using interrupted time series analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the clinical and operational utility of RDTs to optimize the use of newer, targeted antibiotics like C/T. It will quantify the value of diagnostic stewardship in shortening time to effective therapy, potentially improving survival and preserving antibiotic utility. The findings will offer a practical framework for stewardship programs to integrate RDTs for guiding novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor therapy.
proposals.novelty: While RDTs have been studied for carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales, their specific application to guide C/T for MDR P. aeruginosa in a protocolized stewardship context is less defined. This study uniquely focuses on the intersection of a rapid diagnostic, a specific high-priority antibiotic, and a dedicated pharmacist-driven intervention, moving beyond general antibiotic de-escalation to precision escalation.

proposals.title: Feasibility, Safety, and Clinical Outcomes of Ceftolozane-Tazobactam Delivered via Outpatient Parenteral Antibiotic Therapy (OPAT) for Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections: A Multicenter Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections necessitate prolonged intravenous antibiotic courses, placing a significant burden on hospital resources and patient quality of life. Outpatient Parenteral Antibiotic Therapy (OPAT) is a proven model for delivering complex care, but its application for newer, potent agents like ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T) in resistant P. aeruginosa infections remains poorly characterized. Establishing the real-world feasibility and outcomes of C/T-OPAT is critical to inform antimicrobial stewardship and healthcare delivery models, potentially enabling earlier hospital discharge and reducing costs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that C/T delivered via OPAT for MDR P. aeruginosa infections is a feasible, safe, and effective alternative to prolonged inpatient therapy, with comparable clinical cure rates and lower rates of adverse events requiring re-hospitalization.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with MDR P. aeruginosa infections who received C/T via OPAT between 2020-2025. Data will be extracted from electronic health records and OPAT program databases at participating sites (e.g., Michigan Medicine, affiliated with prior work). Primary outcomes are OPAT completion rate and 30-day clinical cure. Secondary outcomes include 30-day readmission, adverse drug events, and OPAT-related complications. Statistical analysis will employ multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of success and failure, adjusting for confounders such as infection source, renal function, and OPAT model (self-administered vs. nurse-administered).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multicenter, real-world evidence on the implementation of C/T in OPAT for a challenging pathogen, directly informing clinical guidelines and stewardship programs. It will quantify the risks and benefits, offering a framework for patient selection and monitoring, thereby supporting the safe expansion of OPAT formularies to include newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While C/T's inpatient efficacy is established (e.g., in the CACTUS study), its systematic evaluation in OPAT is novel. This work bridges the gap between inpatient infectious diseases pharmacotherapy and outpatient care models, a logical extension of Dr. Pogue's research on patient subgroups and antibiotic prescribing patterns.

proposals.title: Integrating Host Immune Biomarkers with Vancomycin Pharmacodynamics to Predict Treatment Outcomes in Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Current vancomycin therapeutic drug monitoring focuses solely on drug exposure (AUC/MIC), yet clinical outcomes remain variable, particularly in complex patients. This variability suggests a critical role for the host immune response, which is not captured in traditional pharmacodynamic models. Integrating patient-specific immune status could improve outcome prediction and personalize dosing strategies, especially for multidrug-resistant infections, a key pre-2026 challenge highlighted in Dr. Pogue's work on MDR Pseudomonas and antibiotic prescribing.
proposals.hypothesis: A pharmacodynamic model that integrates quantitative host immune biomarkers (e.g., neutrophil count, monocyte distribution width, IL-6) with vancomycin exposure metrics will significantly improve the prediction of clinical and microbiological outcomes in patients with S. aureus bacteremia compared to models based on drug exposure alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients with S. aureus bacteremia treated with vancomycin. Using electronic health record data, we will extract serial vancomycin levels, immune biomarkers from routine labs, and clinical outcomes. A population pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) model will be developed using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM v7.5). The base model will characterize vancomycin PK and an exposure-response relationship. Immune biomarkers will be tested as time-varying covariates on parameters for bacterial killing and clinical failure rates, using stepwise covariate modeling and likelihood ratio tests for inclusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrated PK-PD-host model providing a quantitative framework to understand how immune status modifies antibiotic effect. It will offer a proof-of-concept for biomarker-guided antimicrobial therapy, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all exposure target. The findings could inform future prospective trials of personalized vancomycin dosing based on host immune phenotype.
proposals.novelty: While previous models have linked drug exposure to outcome, and others have linked biomarkers to infection severity, this proposal uniquely seeks to formally integrate dynamic immune biomarkers directly into a mechanistic pharmacodynamic model of vancomycin effect. This represents a paradigm shift from a purely drug-centric to a host-drug interaction model.

proposals.title: Site- and Severity-Dependent AUC/MIC Targets: A Population Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Analysis of Beta-Lactam/Beta-Lactamase Inhibitor Combinations in MDR Gram-Negative Infections
proposals.motivation: Current therapeutic drug monitoring for novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, sulbactam-durlobactam) often relies on fixed AUC/MIC targets derived from neutropenic thigh infection models. However, clinical evidence suggests infection site (e.g., pneumonia vs. UTI) and severity (e.g., sepsis) critically impact drug exposure and pharmacodynamics. Applying a universal target may lead to under-dosing in severe infections or unnecessary toxicity in mild ones, a key concern in antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal AUC/MIC target for clinical efficacy of beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations varies significantly based on the anatomical site of infection and the host's inflammatory status/severity of illness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a population pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) analysis using data from phase 3 trials and real-world studies (e.g., CACTUS study data) for ceftazidime-avibactam and ceftolozane-tazobactam. Patient-specific PK parameters will be estimated using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). A logistic regression model will assess the relationship between time-varying AUC/MIC (derived from individual PK estimates and isolate MIC) and clinical cure, with infection site (pneumonia, UTI, bacteremia) and severity markers (e.g., SOFA score, CRP) as effect modifiers. Machine learning (random forest) will identify key covariates driving target variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for personalizing AUC/MIC targets based on clinical phenotype, moving beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' approach. It will generate specific, evidence-based targets for common infection syndromes to guide precision dosing and support antimicrobial stewardship programs.
proposals.novelty: While PK variability is acknowledged, this proposal directly challenges the paradigm of a static PK/PD target by rigorously testing the modulatory effect of infection site and host response, integrating clinical severity scores into PK/PD models for beta-lactamase inhibitors.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of AUC-Guided versus Trough-Guided Vancomycin Dosing on Clinical Cure and Nephrotoxicity in Patients with Serious MRSA Infections: A Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin remains a cornerstone for treating serious methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections. However, traditional trough-based dosing is associated with significant nephrotoxicity risk, and its correlation with the pharmacodynamic target of AUC/MIC is poor. Emerging pre-2026 guidelines and studies advocate for AUC-based dosing to improve efficacy and safety, but large-scale, real-world comparative effectiveness data on patient-centered outcomes are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AUC-guided vancomycin dosing, compared to traditional trough-guided dosing, will be associated with a higher rate of clinical cure and a lower incidence of nephrotoxicity in patients with serious MRSA infections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2025. Adult inpatients treated for MRSA bacteremia or pneumonia with ≥72 hours of vancomycin will be included. Patients will be stratified into AUC-guided (dosing managed via Bayesian software or first-order pharmacokinetic equations targeting an AUC of 400-600 mg·h/L) and trough-guided (targeting 10-20 mg/L) cohorts. The primary outcome is a composite of clinical cure (resolution of signs/symptoms without relapse) and 30-day all-cause mortality. The key safety outcome is nephrotoxicity, defined by the 2020 vancomycin consensus guidelines. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders including age, comorbidities, baseline renal function, and concomitant nephrotoxins.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the impact of a fundamental dosing paradigm shift on patient outcomes. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs and guideline committees by quantifying the potential benefits of AUC-based dosing on both efficacy and toxicity endpoints. The findings will support more precise, personalized vancomycin therapy to improve patient safety and treatment success.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have focused on pharmacokinetic target attainment or nephrotoxicity alone, this study concurrently evaluates the dual endpoints of clinical cure and nephrotoxicity in a large, diverse patient cohort, providing a holistic assessment of the comparative effectiveness of this dosing strategy change.

ideas.title: Standardized immunomodulator trials for bacterial sepsis using a consensus-driven endpoint framework
ideas.description: This research applies a modified Delphi process to first develop standardized, consensus-based primary outcome definitions for clinical trials in specific bacterial infections, such as Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia. It then uses these newly standardized endpoints to design and directly compare the efficacy of different immunomodulators (e.g., tocilizumab vs. JAK inhibitors) as adjunctive therapies in these infections. This approach solves the problem of heterogeneous trial outcomes that currently hinder meta-analyses and cross-infection comparisons. The result is a generalizable framework for rationally evaluating host-directed therapies across diverse sepsis etiologies, accelerating the identification of optimal immunomodulatory strategies for specific pathogens.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to Pathogen Management
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the universal assumption that infections with pathogens like Pseudomonas or antibiotic-resistant organisms always require complex, aggressive management and cannot be considered uncomplicated. Drawing an analogy from ecology's 'novel ecosystems' framework—which solved the problem of blanket eradication mandates for non-native species by developing criteria for their functional integration—this idea adapts that solution to infectious diseases. The adapted research would develop and validate a clinical decision framework using specific, measurable parameters (e.g., low pathogen burden, absence of high-risk virulence genes, favorable host response, non-critical infection site) to identify a subset of such infections. The goal is to define evidence-based scenarios where these pathogens can be managed as 'uncomplicated' with specific, streamlined antimicrobial regimens, potentially improving antibiotic stewardship and patient outcomes.

The research employs an adaptive, multi-center trial design with embedded, real-time safety monitoring to identify early signals of failure, triggering protocol-defined escalation to source control. This approach ensures the evaluation is both clinically meaningful and ethically sound, moving beyond short-term stability to assess genuine, durable treatment success.

ideas.title: Adaptive Platform Trial to Validate and Dynamically Refine Consensus Definitions for Severe Infection Syndromes
ideas.description: This research proposes a Bayesian adaptive platform trial for severe infections (e.g., COVID-19, sepsis from GNBSI) that integrates the prospective validation of consensus syndromic definitions as a core, adaptive component. The trial would simultaneously test multiple immunomodulators or other therapeutics while using accumulating real-world data to test the prognostic and predictive utility of the proposed 'uncomplicated' vs. 'complicated' infection definitions. Patient stratification and treatment assignment rules would be dynamically updated based on which definitional criteria best predict outcomes and treatment response, creating a continuous feedback loop between syndromic phenotyping and therapeutic evaluation. This synergy ensures that therapeutic efficacy is measured in optimally defined, clinically meaningful patient cohorts, accelerating both precise treatment discovery and the evolution of disease taxonomy.

This approach eliminates the reliance on pre-defined, expert-consensus criteria for long-term outcomes, which are often lacking. The models will be trained to identify complex, temporally extended risk phenotypes that manifest weeks or months after hospital discharge, creating a scalable tool for post-market safety surveillance. This enables a continuous, data-driven assessment of the long-term infection risk profile associated with immunomodulatory therapies in real-world populations.

The analysis will model clinical and economic outcomes of implementing consensus-style de-escalation protocols triggered by bacterial biomarkers, compared to universal IL-6 blockade. This determines not only if such a personalized strategy improves patient safety, but whether it is economically justified for healthcare systems.

ideas.title: Causal Inference for Outpatient Pathway Safety: Robustly Evaluating the Real-World Effectiveness of Ambulatory GNBSI Management
ideas.description: This research fuses the development of outpatient protocols for uncomplicated Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) with advanced causal inference methods to rigorously evaluate their real-world safety and efficacy. The core aim is to use techniques like instrumental variable analysis, g-computation, or doubly robust estimation to adjust for the significant confounding by indication inherent in selecting patients for outpatient management. This approach will generate robust, bias-adjusted estimates of the causal effect of the outpatient pathway on clinical outcomes (e.g., treatment failure, relapse, mortality) and healthcare utilization compared to standard inpatient care. The resulting evidence will not only validate the safety of the protocol but also provide a methodological framework for the robust evaluation of other novel healthcare delivery interventions where randomization is impractical.

ideas.description: This study moves beyond a simple drug-versus-drug comparison by first classifying hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 into distinct immune phenotypes using a predefined panel of biomarkers and clinical features. Within an adaptive platform trial framework, patients are then randomized to immunomodulator therapies (including tocilizumab and JAK inhibitors) selected based on their phenotype, or to standard of care. The primary aim is to determine if phenotype-targeted therapy improves survival and clinical outcomes compared to non-targeted care, thereby testing the central hypothesis that patient stratification is essential for effective immunomodulation. The adaptive design allows for early dropping of ineffective strategies and introduction of new ones based on interim analyses.

ideas.title: Human-Centered, Adaptive Clinical Decision Support for GNBSI Using Real-Time, Data-Driven Phenotyping
ideas.description: This research synthesizes human factors engineering and data-driven machine learning to develop a novel clinical decision support (CDS) system for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). Instead of implementing a fixed consensus definition or a purely data-mined model, the system uses human-centered design to present a machine-learning-generated phenotype of 'uncomplicated' infection within the EHR workflow, actively capturing clinician overrides and reasoning. These human interactions, combined with patient outcomes, are then fed back as training data to iteratively refine the predictive model, creating a continuous learning cycle. The result is an adaptive CDS tool that respects clinical expertise, improves through use, and personalizes guidance based on local practice patterns and patient populations.

ideas.title: Adapting ecological network resilience modeling to simulate antibiotic guideline impact
ideas.description: This research adapts agent-based modeling (ABM) techniques from ecology, where they are used to simulate policy impacts on ecosystem resilience, to the hospital epidemiology context. The analogous ecological solution involves modeling individual species within a food web to predict how conservation policies affect network stability and biodiversity. Here, we adapt this by constructing an ABM where the agents are patients (with states of colonization/infection), healthcare workers (with prescription and hygiene behaviors), and bacterial populations (with traits for resistance and fitness). The consensus antibiotic guideline is implemented as a change in the prescribing rules for the healthcare worker agents. The model simulates the hospital-wide 'ecosystem' over time, measuring how the policy intervention ripples through the contact network, altering the competitive landscape for bacterial agents. The output assesses the guideline's impact on emergent resistance patterns and infection outbreaks, providing a dynamic, systems-level forecast of the policy's effect on the hospital's microbial ecology resilience, directly parallel to predicting a conservation policy's effect on ecosystem health.

ideas.title: Defining 'Complicated' GNBSI Through Ecological Succession Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology and ecosystem management to the clinical problem of defining and managing 'complicated' Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections (GNBSI). In ecology, ecosystems are not simply 'disturbed' or 'healthy'; they are classified into successive stages (e.g., primary vs. secondary succession) based on the severity of disruption to foundational structures like soil or keystone species. Management protocols are then explicitly tiered, with interventions such as replanting schemes or predator reintroduction carefully scaled to the specific stage's complexity and required recovery timeline. We adapt this framework to systematically define 'complicated' GNBSI. We propose moving beyond a vague binary classification to establish a staged model (e.g., Stage 1: Uncomplicated bacteremia; Stage 2: Metastatic focus; Stage 3: Persisting source with biofilm; Stage 4: Immune-dysregulated persistence). For each defined stage, parallel consensus guidance will be created, specifying the appropriate scaling of diagnostic investigations (e.g., when to use advanced imaging), antimicrobial strategy (including duration, need for combination therapy, and consideration of biofilm-active agents), and source control intensity. This creates a structured, scalable management protocol analogous to ecosystem recovery plans, providing clear, stage-appropriate pathways for the longer, more complex management these infections require.

ideas.title: Adaptive Platform Trial for GNBSI with Multi-Armed Bandit Allocation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from machine learning's multi-armed bandit problem to the clinical trial design for Gram-negative bloodstream infection (GNBSI) therapy. In the analogous field, algorithms dynamically allocate resources (like web traffic) to the most promising options (arms) based on continuous feedback to maximize a reward. Adapted to the adaptive platform trial context, this means implementing a response-adaptive randomization algorithm where the probability of assigning a new patient to a specific antibiotic duration or oral switch strategy is automatically adjusted based on accumulating interim data (e.g., clinical cure rates, relapse, adverse events). This efficiently steers more participants toward regimens that appear more effective or safer, accelerating the identification of optimal therapies while maintaining the trial's ability to generate robust comparative evidence across all strategies simultaneously.

ideas.title: Medical Watchful Waiting: Adapting Oncology's Active Surveillance Protocol to Uncomplicated Infections
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the assumption that source control procedurally precedes definition in infection management. Drawing an analogy from oncology, specifically the paradigm shift in managing low-risk prostate cancer from immediate definitive treatment to Active Surveillance, we adapt this solution. In oncology, patients are closely monitored with scheduled tests, and intervention is reserved for signs of disease progression, successfully avoiding overtreatment. Adapted to infectious disease, the idea is to study if certain infections currently labeled as 'uncomplicated' can be managed with a structured 'Medical Watchful Waiting' protocol instead of immediate source control. This would involve defining specific infection types (e.g., select diverticulitis cases), establishing a monitoring protocol with serial clinical exams, biomarkers (like CRP, procalcitonin), and imaging, and setting clear criteria for intervention. The research would test the hypothesis that a subset of patients will resolve without procedural source control, thereby challenging the sequence of care and potentially reducing procedural morbidity and healthcare costs.

ideas.title: A Globally-Adaptive Decision Support Framework for Infectious Disease Management
ideas.description: This research synthesizes a multi-national Delphi consensus process with formal decision-analytic modeling to create an adaptive framework for infectious disease management. The Delphi study gathers structured expert input on regional variations in pathogens, antimicrobial resistance, and resource constraints from a global panel. These inputs are then used to parameterize and weight branches in decision trees or states in Markov models, transforming qualitative consensus into quantitative, optimizable pathways. The resulting framework can generate and compare evidence-based management algorithms that are specifically tailored to different regional contexts, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all guideline to a suite of optimized, implementable strategies.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecosystem Stability Models to Compare Immunomodulator Efficacy in COVID-19
ideas.description: This future work proposes to evaluate tocilizumab against other anti-cytokine therapies like JAK inhibitors in severe COVID-19 by drawing an analogy from ecology and ecosystem stability theory. In that field, the stability of a system under stress is solved by analyzing multiple, interacting components like resistance, resilience, and functional redundancy under different perturbations. The adapted idea applies this multi-component framework to immunology. The research would design a clinical trial or analysis to compare these immunomodulators not on a single endpoint, but by measuring parallel 'immune stability' components in patients: resistance to cytokine storm escalation, resilience in inflammatory marker recovery, and functional redundancy of preserved immune pathways. This approach aims to determine which therapeutic strategy provides the most robust and stable rebalancing of the dysregulated immune response in severe COVID-19.

ideas.title: Global Delphi Consensus on Pathogen-Specific Hyperinflammatory Endotypes Using CAR-T CRS as a Model Framework
ideas.description: This study initiates a global, multi-round Delphi process involving immunologists, intensivists, and virologists from diverse geographic regions. The core task for the expert panel is to use the well-characterized IL-6 biology and cytokine cascade of CAR-T cell Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) as a foundational model to compare, contrast, and define distinct hyperinflammatory endotypes triggered by different regional pathogens (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, influenza, dengue, bacterial sepsis). The goal is to achieve an international consensus on the fundamental differences in immune dysregulation, which will directly inform the development of regionally-tailored diagnostic criteria and stratified anti-cytokine therapy protocols, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach to cytokine storm management.

ideas.title: Agent-based simulation of tocilizumab timing and dosing strategies on hospital-wide critical care resource use and patient outcomes
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based model (ABM) of a hospital's critical care pathway to simulate the impact of different tocilizumab administration policies. The ABM incorporates individual patient agents with varying disease trajectories, where the timing of tocilizumab (pre- or post-intubation) influences their probability of requiring mechanical ventilation, length of ICU stay, and outcome. By simulating the adoption of different timing guidelines across a simulated hospital population over time, the model can forecast system-wide outcomes, including ventilator shortages, bed occupancy, and aggregate survival rates, thereby identifying policies that optimize both individual patient care and institutional resource resilience.

ideas.title: Adapt a Seismic Hazard Assessment Framework to Stratify Risk in Gram-Negative Bacteremia
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology from probabilistic seismic hazard assessment (PSHA) in geophysics to the problem of risk-stratifying patients with gram-negative bacteremia. In seismology, disparate data streams (historical seismicity, fault slip rates, soil conditions) are integrated into a unified probabilistic model to create hazard maps that define zones of risk. Analogously, we propose to develop a clinical risk stratification tool by creating a probabilistic model that integrates multi-dimensional patient data—including host response biomarkers (e.g., procalcitonin, lactate), pathogen-specific factors (e.g., species, resistance profile), and clinical course metrics (e.g., time to defervescence, source control)—into a unified scoring algorithm. The output will be a validated, objective 'bacteremia hazard score' that estimates the probability of complications, thereby clearly identifying a low-risk cohort suitable for early oral step-down therapy or shorter treatment, optimizing resource use and patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Modeling the Economic Impact of Streamlined Protocols using Manufacturing Process Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from industrial engineering and lean manufacturing to assess the economic impact of clinical consensus recommendations. The analogous problem—optimizing a manufacturing process by reducing cycle times and eliminating non-value-added steps—is solved using value stream mapping and discrete-event simulation to model cost and throughput changes. Adapted to the medical context, the idea is to build a simulation model of the current care pathway for bloodstream infections. By inputting the proposed changes (shorter therapy duration, elimination of routine repeat blood cultures) as streamlined protocol parameters, the model would generate a comprehensive economic analysis. This would capture not only direct cost savings from reduced drug use and lab tests but also indirect benefits from improved resource utilization (e.g., bed turnover, staff time), providing a robust, system-wide assessment of cost-effectiveness to inform implementation decisions.

ideas.description: This study employs a multi-arm, adaptive platform trial design to test whether IL-6 receptor blockade (e.g., tocilizumab) is beneficial or harmful in severe COVID-19 based on a dynamically learned, integrative patient phenotype. Patients are continuously assigned using response-adaptive randomization, with allocation probabilities updated by a Bayesian model that synthesizes high-frequency data on immune cell profiles, bacterial load, viral dynamics, and end-organ injury. The primary outcome is to identify and validate a composite 'treatable trait' signature that predicts treatment response, moving beyond static subgroup definitions. This approach directly addresses pathophysiological heterogeneity and aims to generate a clinically actionable decision rule for targeted immunomodulation.

ideas.title: A Phase-Transition Model for Tocilizumab Timing: Applying Critical Window Analysis from Ischemic Stroke Thrombolysis
ideas.description: This research seed applies an analogical solution from neurology, specifically the timing-critical administration of thrombolytics in ischemic stroke. The analogous problem—determining an optimal treatment window relative to a pathophysiological transition point—was solved by conducting clinical trials that correlated outcomes with precise treatment timing to establish evidence-based guidelines. Adapted to this context, the study would investigate tocilizumab timing relative to mechanical ventilation initiation by conceptualizing intubation as a clinical phase transition analogous to stroke onset. The goal is to determine if a 'critical window' exists—either pre-intubation to prevent progression or post-intubation to mitigate established injury—by analyzing outcomes against a continuous measure of time-to-treatment from this event, thereby defining the optimal timing for immunomodulator administration.

ideas.title: Modeling the Implementation Cost-Effectiveness of Shorter Therapy and No-Repeat Protocols
ideas.description: This study evaluates the full economic impact of adopting consensus recommendations by integrating implementation science into the cost-effectiveness analysis. It first conducts a baseline assessment to identify barriers to adherence and estimates the costs of different implementation strategies required to change practice. Using a hybrid simulation model, it then compares the net cost-effectiveness of the new clinical protocol when combined with various implementation approaches, capturing the true total cost of achieving and sustaining high-fidelity adoption in real-world settings.

ideas.title: Prospective validation of a clinical consensus definition using a phased implementation framework from software engineering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the phased testing and release framework from software engineering to validate a clinical consensus definition. The analogous solution involves staged releases (alpha, beta, stable) to iteratively test software in environments of increasing complexity and scale. Adapted to the clinical research context, the proposed study would prospectively test the definition of uncomplicated GNBSI and its management recommendations not in one monolithic cohort, but through sequential, phased implementation. Starting with a tightly controlled 'alpha' phase in a single unit, followed by a 'beta' phase across a limited hospital network, and culminating in a broad 'stable' phase, this approach allows for real-time validation, identification of contextual barriers, and iterative refinement of the definition and recommendations based on real-world data before full-scale adoption is recommended.

ideas.title: Validate ordinal scale via a multi-center, multi-pathogen consortium protocol
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to validate the novel ordinal outcome scale for superinfections by drawing an analogy from ecology, where new biodiversity assessment scales are validated across diverse sites. The analogous solution involves standardized, multi-site monitoring protocols with shared toolkits and centralized training. Adapted to the clinical research context, this means establishing a multi-center consortium that implements a standardized validation protocol. This protocol would include a manual of operations for scale application, centralized training for site investigators to ensure consistent adjudication of ordinal outcomes, and the use of statistical models to account for between-center heterogeneity. The goal is to rigorously test the scale's reliability, validity, and generalizability across independent, diverse patient cohorts and healthcare settings.

ideas.title: Testing Propensity Score Validity with a 'Synthetic Control' Approach from Econometrics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that propensity scoring fully adjusts for confounding by drawing an analogy from econometric program evaluation. In that field, the synthetic control method is used to construct a credible counterfactual for assessing policy impacts by matching pre-intervention trends. We adapt this solution to the biomedical context. The adapted research idea involves selecting a patient subgroup where the clinical indication for a treatment is so clear that their expected outcome trajectory without treatment is well-understood (e.g., a predictable disease progression). For these patients, we use the propensity score model to create a synthetic control cohort from patients who did not receive the treatment but have similar measured covariates. If the propensity score model is perfect, the outcomes in this synthetic cohort should match the known natural history. Significant deviations would indicate residual confounding due to model misspecification or unmeasured variables, providing a direct test of the propensity score's validity for a given dataset and research question.

ideas.title: Adaptive Consensus Framework for Global Pathogen Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the consensus-building methodology of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) to the challenge of establishing a global, multi-national consensus on pathogen surveillance and antimicrobial resistance. The analogous solution from climate science involves a structured, iterative process synthesizing regional data through expert working groups, transparent review cycles, and tiered confidence assessments. Adapted to the biomedical context, the research would establish a standing international expert consortium—modeled on the IPCC—to conduct ongoing, iterative consensus exercises. This consortium would systematically integrate regional variations in pathogens, resistance patterns, and healthcare resources, moving beyond a one-time Delphi study to create a dynamic, living consensus framework. The output would be regularly updated global guidance documents that explicitly rate the confidence and applicability of recommendations for different world regions, thereby scaling consensus to a sustainable, institutionalized global system.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Net Benefit of Immunosuppression: A Cost-Benefit Framework from Conservation Biology
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from conservation biology, where managing trade-offs between saving a target species and causing collateral ecosystem damage is routine. The analogous problem is solved using quantitative decision frameworks that model primary benefits against secondary cascading costs. Adapted to the original medical idea, this involves creating a formal model for immunosuppressive therapies. The model would treat survival/mortality reduction as the primary conservation benefit and the increased incidence, severity, and morbidity of superinfections as the accepted ecological cost. The output is a patient-stratified, quantifiable 'net clinical benefit' metric, balancing these competing outcomes to inform when the trade-off is acceptable and for which patient profiles, moving the assumption from implicit to explicitly quantified.

ideas.title: Early Warning System for GNBSI Complication Using Predictive Failure Signatures
ideas.description: This research seed expands via analogy to structural engineering's solution for predicting material failure. The analogous problem—detecting microscopic cracks that predict catastrophic structural failure—is solved by monitoring early warning signals like acoustic emission signatures and strain patterns. Adapted to the clinical context of Gram-negative bloodstream infection (GNBSI), the idea is to identify early predictive signatures of complication. By analyzing dynamic, serial patterns in easily obtainable clinical data (vitals, routine labs) and host-response biomarkers, the research aims to define a 'failure signature' that signals when a patient's presumed uncomplicated infection is on a trajectory to become complicated (e.g., developing persistent bacteremia, metastatic foci, or sepsis). This creates an early warning system, scaling the dual problem of management by distinguishing patients who will remain uncomplicated from those requiring more aggressive intervention.
