ideas.title: Applying Ecological Trophic Cascade Analysis to Identify Harmful Subgroups in IL-6 Blockade
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that IL-6 blockade is universally beneficial in severe COVID-19 by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecological systems, removing a keystone species (analogous to blocking IL-6) can destabilize specific subsystems within a complex network, rather than having a uniformly positive effect. The analogous solution involves modeling the entire interaction network (food web) to predict harmful outcomes. Adapted to COVID-19, the research would use multi-omic and clinical data from severe patients to construct a high-resolution model of the immune-inflammatory 'ecosystem.' This model would be used to simulate the effects of IL-6 blockade and identify patient subgroups (e.g., those with specific immune-bacterial co-infection states) where this intervention disrupts essential immune coordination, potentially increasing the risk of secondary infection or organ failure, thereby testing and refining the initial universal benefit hypothesis.

ideas.title: Long-term ecological monitoring of immune disruption after targeted immunosuppression
ideas.description: This research adapts the long-term ecological monitoring approach from conservation biology to assess superinfection risk post-tocilizumab. Just as ecologists track an ecosystem's recovery and new vulnerabilities for decades after a major perturbation, this study would establish a prospective, longitudinal patient registry to monitor the 'immune ecosystem' long after the initial immunosuppressive event. The adapted solution involves tracking patients like permanent ecological plots, systematically collecting data on clinical infections, latent virus reactivation, microbiome composition, and immune cell repertoire at regular intervals over 5-10 years. This aims to identify delayed, cascading risks and define the long-term trajectory of immune resilience and vulnerability following targeted interleukin-6 receptor blockade.

ideas.title: Adapting Automated Taxonomy Generation from Ecology to Define Uncomplicated GNBSI
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a formal expert consensus definition is a prerequisite for developing predictive models for uncomplicated GNBSI. Drawing an analogy from computational ecology, where data-driven models now define ecological categories directly from field data, we propose a similar methodological shift. We will develop machine learning models that process granular electronic health record data—including temporal sequences of vitals, laboratory results, antimicrobial administrations, and clinical outcomes—to identify the multivariate signature of a straightforward infection course. This approach allows the data itself to define the 'uncomplicated' phenotype in an objective, reproducible, and potentially more granular way than a consensus guideline, creating a model that can both define and predict the condition simultaneously.

ideas.title: Phenotype-Adaptive Platform Trial for COVID-19 Immunomodulators with Integrated Biomarker Learning
ideas.description: This design implements a Bayesian adaptive platform trial specifically engineered to address the profound heterogeneity of critical COVID-19. It incorporates a mandatory high-dimensional biomarker assessment at enrollment to classify patients into evolving phenotypic strata. The trial's core adaptive algorithm not only evaluates treatments but continuously learns and refines the biomarker definitions that predict response or harm to each immunomodulator. This ensures the right therapeutic mechanism is matched to the right patient biology, maximizing the chance of detecting efficacy and generating clinically actionable precision-medicine rules for patient stratification in real time.

ideas.title: Technology-Enabled Dynamic Risk Stratification and Monitoring for Outpatient GNBSI Management
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a bundled system for safe outpatient GNBSI management, moving beyond static protocols. It first creates and implements a point-of-care risk-stratification tool using clinical data and a host-response biomarker to identify truly low-risk patients. The core intervention is a mandatory, time-limited (96-hour) intensive monitoring phase using a digital platform for daily symptom reporting and vital sign entry, coupled with structured telehealth assessments. The system includes explicit failure criteria and a streamlined pathway for immediate reversion to inpatient care, ensuring safety by actively detecting early deterioration rather than assuming it will not occur.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Likelihood Models to Clinical Decision Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from evolutionary biology, where phylogenetic likelihood models are used to infer the most probable evolutionary tree from genetic sequence data. The analogous problem is determining the most probable historical pathway of evolution, solved by defining models of evolutionary change and computationally evaluating their likelihood given observed data. Adapted to clinical management, we formalize consensus criteria and possible decisions into a branching decision tree structure. We then define a likelihood model that calculates the probability of observed longitudinal patient data (trajectories and outcomes) under different instantiations of the management pathway (including decision points and transition probabilities). Using optimization techniques from phylogenetics (e.g., tree space search algorithms), we can infer the decision pathway model that has the maximum likelihood of producing the real-world clinical dataset, thereby data-optimizing management protocols.

ideas.title: Outpatient GNBSI Management via Telemedicine-Enabled Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'lights-out' mission control solution from remote sensing and satellite operations to the challenge of scaling down GNBSI management. In satellite operations, automated telemetry and anomaly detection systems replaced constant human oversight for routine missions, allowing remote management. Adapted here, the idea is to develop integrated protocols and a telemedicine platform with a robust clinical decision support system (CDSS) for managing select uncomplicated Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) entirely in the outpatient setting. The CDSS would act as the automated 'ground station,' processing inputs from remote patient monitoring devices and electronic health records to ensure adherence to outpatient treatment protocols, flag only concerning trends for specialist review, and thereby safely enable a shift from resource-intensive inpatient care to efficient, scalable outpatient management.

ideas.title: Human-Centered, Adaptive Clinical Decision Support Powered by a Living Delphi Consensus Engine
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated platform that connects a web-based, continuous Delphi system to EHR-embedded clinical decision support (CDS) via human factors engineering principles. The living Delphi panel continuously synthesizes new study data into updated consensus statements, which are then automatically formatted into CDS logic. Human-centered design is applied not just to the CDS interface, but to the entire cycle—designing how consensus changes are communicated to clinicians, how feedback from clinical use is captured, and how this feedback is fed back to the Delphi panel to prioritize evidence gaps. The result is a learning health system for guidelines that adapts both to new evidence and to the practical needs and behaviors of frontline clinicians.

ideas.title: Applying Seismology's Aftershock Forecasting to Model IL-6 Dynamics in Cytokine Storms
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of fundamental differences in IL-6 biology between COVID-19 and CAR-T cell therapy by proposing they may share a common dynamical pattern. Drawing an analogy from seismology, where the Epidemic-Type Aftershock Sequence (ETAS) model successfully forecasts the rate and decay of aftershocks following a main earthquake, we adapt this solution to immunology. The adapted idea is to model the initial cytokine trigger (CAR-T cell engagement or viral infection) as the 'main shock.' Subsequent IL-6 release events from monocytes, macrophages, and T cells are treated as 'aftershocks,' whose probability and magnitude decay over time according to a quantifiable statistical law. By applying this temporal clustering analysis to longitudinal patient IL-6 data from both CAR-T and COVID-19 cohorts, the research would test whether the hyperinflammatory trajectories are governed by similar trigger-dependent decay dynamics rather than distinct biological mechanisms. A positive finding would unify the understanding of cytokine storms across etiologies and could lead to predictive models for clinical deterioration based on early cytokine 'seismicity.'

ideas.title: Comparative Systems Immunology of Hyperinflammation: Decoupling Shared Circuitry from Disease-Specific Drivers in CAR-T CRS and Severe COVID-19
ideas.description: This project systematically compares the hyperinflammatory states in CAR-T cell therapy CRS and severe COVID-19 without assuming one fully models the other. Using longitudinal single-cell multi-omics on patient samples, it will generate high-resolution maps of immune cell states, signaling pathways, and cytokine networks for each condition. A computational integration model will then distinguish a conserved 'hyperinflammation core' from unique, disease-origin-specific immune modules. The final phase will experimentally perturb these identified pathways in primary human cell systems to validate their functional roles and therapeutic relevance for each distinct clinical context.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Evidence-Integrated Decision Pathways via Online Learning and Regret Minimization
ideas.description: This system creates a self-correcting management pathway optimizer that formally integrates initial expert consensus with continuous real-world evidence. It employs an online learning framework where the decision tree or Markov model is not static but is treated as a hypothesis that is tested against incoming operational or clinical data. The system identifies regions of high 'regret'—where model recommendations consistently underperform compared to observed alternative actions—and triggers model refinement or targeted data collection. This results in management pathways that are both principled and pragmatically adapted to the evolving context and heterogeneity of the deployment environment.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery-Guided Robustness Testing for Treatment Effect Estimation
ideas.description: This approach first employs causal discovery algorithms on observed pre-treatment data to learn a set of plausible causal Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) representing the confounding structure. It then uses this structural knowledge to formally assess key assumptions like the absence of unmeasured confounding for exposure-outcome pairs and the suitability of instruments. Finally, it executes a targeted suite of causal inference methods (e.g., propensity score, g-formula, instrumental variable) only where their required assumptions align with the discovered graphs, and quantifies robustness through sensitivity analyses that vary the strength of potential unmeasured confounding.

The analogous CRM solution is adapted to the healthcare context by using human-centered design to integrate a clinical guideline's consensus definition into EHR clinical decision support. The adapted research idea is to apply CRM principles to design the CDS interface and workflow, focusing on creating shared mental models, embedding structured communication protocols (e.g., SBAR) within the CDS, and managing cognitive load through context-aware prioritization and timing of guideline prompts, thereby improving the effectiveness and adoption of the implemented guideline.

ideas.title: A Cognitive Workflow Orchestration System for Dynamic Guideline Implementation
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a system that moves beyond human-centered design for individual alerts to a holistic, adaptive management layer for clinical guidance. It uses real-time sensors and models of individual and team cognitive load, task context, and the evolving evidence landscape to intelligently route and format guideline-based support. The system dynamically arbitrates between competing alerts, chooses optimal delivery channels (e.g., EHR vs. secure chat), and can defer non-critical notifications to reduce interruption, thereby embedding guideline support into a coherent, system-aware workflow rather than as isolated CDS artifacts.

ideas.title: Adversarial, Convergence-Resistant Delphi with Synthetic Challenge Cycles
ideas.description: This system transforms the continuous Delphi into a dynamic, multi-track process designed to combat groupthink and premature consensus. Multiple independent expert panels deliberate in parallel, with their anonymized rationales and interim conclusions periodically shared across panels to introduce contradictory perspectives without social pressure. The system algorithmically generates 'synthetic challenge' cycles, presenting panels with tailored counter-arguments and hypothetical disconfirming evidence to test the robustness of their positions. A final consensus stage only occurs after these adversarial cycles, ensuring the resulting judgment is resilient and has been rigorously stress-tested against alternative interpretations.

ideas.title: Operational stress-testing through distributed simulation for global guideline development
ideas.description: This approach establishes a network of regional simulation hubs that run concurrent, data-driven exercises mirroring local health systems. Expert panels participate in these simulations, observing how draft consensus guidelines fail under modeled constraints like budget cycles, supply chain fragility, or staffing shortages. The insights from these simulated failures are used to revise the guidelines iteratively, ensuring the final multinational consensus is pre-validated against operational realities across different contexts. This creates a pragmatic, implementable framework rather than a purely theoretical agreement.

ideas.title: A Bayesian Adaptive Delphi System with Real-Time Evidence Assimilation
ideas.description: This research proposes a 'Bayesian Adaptive Delphi System' inspired by real-time ensemble forecasting in meteorology. In weather prediction, multiple models are continuously updated with new data and their outputs aggregated. Adapted to the Delphi context, each expert panelist functions as a unique model providing a prior probability distribution for a given research question. A central algorithm, acting as the data assimilation engine, ingests new study findings (treated as likelihoods) and updates a master Bayesian consensus estimate in real-time. Instead of simple periodic re-voting, the system triggers targeted, asynchronous re-evaluation requests to specific panelists when the variance of the collective posterior distribution increases beyond a set threshold, indicating heightened uncertainty in light of new evidence. This creates a living, evidence-driven consensus that is dynamically refined, optimizing expert engagement and maintaining an always-current state of collective judgment.
